source.title: Traffic Control SDK: API contracts for canary, A/B, shadowing and auto-rollback
source.creator: Priya Nair
source.description: Define and finalize the public API and SDK client helper contracts for traffic control use-cases: canary rollouts, A/B tests, shadow traffic, and automated rollback. This ticket scopes server endpoints, client-side idempotency and retry semantics, RBAC/permission surfaces, audit logging expectations, and the metric/signals model used to trigger auto-rollback decisions. Goal is a stable contract that can be implemented by engineering and consumed by internal platform teams and external SDK users.
acceptance_criteria: API surface doc describing endpoints for creating/updating rollout configurations (createRollout, updateRollout, getRollout, pauseRollout, finalizeRollout)
SDK client helpers with idempotent create/update semantics and conflict handling guidance (optimistic tokens + server-assigned rollout_id)
Clear permission model: who can create canaries, who can shadow, who can trigger manual rollback; mapping to existing RBAC roles and audit events
Auto-rollback signal spec: list of metrics (latency P95, error-rate, quality-regression delta), aggregation window, thresholds and hysteresis rules
Telemetry contract for rollout lifecycle events (rollout.started, rollout.updated, rollout.traffic_changed, rollout.rollback_initiated, rollout.completed) with payload schema and retention guidance
Docs and example code snippets in SDKs (Python/Node/Go) demonstrating common patterns: canary then scale, A/B metric comparison, shadow router setup, and manual vs automatic rollback flows
Security review completed and sign-off from Privacy & Legal for audit log retention and data fields
Backward-compatible versioning guidance: how to evolve rollout configs without breaking existing consumers
notes: Proposal will aim to minimize surface area: one primary Rollout resource + typed traffic strategies (CANARY, AB, SHADOW).
Consider server-side idempotency tokens for create operations; client helpers should surface a stable operation idempotencyKey and retry policy.
Auto-rollback should be configurable per-rollout with default conservative thresholds to avoid flapping.
Need to align on cost controls: shadow traffic should be rate-limited and flagged to billing/observability to avoid unexpected spend.
Plan to provide a feature-flagged beta for external customers on Dedicated tier before public GA.
Potential trade-off: richer rollout DSL vs. simpler primitives. Recommend simpler primitives plus composition helpers in SDKs for v1.
comments: 2026-02-21 - Priya Nair: Kickoff notes attached. Asked Eng to sketch minimal endpoint shapes and error model.

2026-03-01 - Mateo Alvarez: Draft API sketch received from Eng (see PR). Need to decide on using server-assigned rollout_id vs client-supplied opaque id. Engineering prefers server-assigned to avoid collisions.
2026-03-05 - Emily Chen (Eng): Recommending optimistic concurrency using an etag field on Rollout resource. Server will reject updates with mismatched etag (409) and return latest state.
2026-03-08 - Jordan Kim (Security): RBAC mapping looks OK, but we need explicit audit fields for shadow traffic payloads to avoid PII leakage.
2026-03-12 - Priya Nair: Updated ACs to include telemetry contract and security review. PM to confirm default auto-rollback thresholds.
2026-03-14 - Mateo Alvarez: Beginning writing SDK helper prototypes for Python. Will include high-level convenience methods: start_canary(), scale_canary(), compare_ab_metrics(), enable_shadow().

source.title: Q3 Synced Roadmap: Hosted API Feature Phasing & Model Catalog Convergence
source.creator: Maya Chen
source.description: Quarterly planning ticket to align Hosted API feature phases with model catalog transitions for Q3. Objective is to produce a sequenced rollout plan that minimizes runtime risk, keeps SLAs intact, and preserves predictable unit economics while enabling new model variants in the catalog. This ticket coordinates cross-team decisions (runtime, infra, design, sales) and produces the final phased milestone plan for Product and Eng to execute. Scope includes feature gating, phasing of model variants, compatibility guardrails, customer migration guidance, and OKR mapping. Out of scope: Dedicated and Private-specific rollout mechanics (handled in separate tickets).
acceptance_criteria: Finalized phase schedule (canary -> staged -> GA) for all Hosted API features and model catalog entries targeted for Q3
Signed-off rollout checklist covering SLA targets, fallback policies, and customer communication templates
Mapped dependencies to engineering tickets with owners and dates (>=90% of dependencies have owners)
Updated OKR/initiative tracker with measurable milestones and owners
Playbook entry created in Confluence for migration and rollback with runbook links
updates: 2026-03-03 - Kickoff: Initial framing meeting with PM, Eng leads (runtime, infra) and Design. Agreed on goals and scope. Assigned primary owners for phases.
2026-03-06 - Discovery: Runtime team provided latency/cost impact matrix for candidate model variants. Noted two models likely require quantization work before GA.
2026-03-09 - Cross-functional sync: Sales highlighted three enterprise customers with SLO constraints; flagged as high-priority migrations.
2026-03-11 - Draft phase schedule completed: proposed canary windows and progressive traffic percentages. Shared for feedback with Eng and Design.
2026-03-12 - Risk review: Identified capacity constraint in eu-west-1 during peak hours; dependency logged to ENG-2319 for autoscaling adjustments.
2026-03-14 - PM review: Incorporated feedback from Design on customer-facing docs. Preparing final milestone mapping for OKR tracker; next step is stakeholder approval meeting on 2026-03-17.

source.title: Hybrid-reserved GPU fit & SLA triage playbook for Dedicated intake
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Summary: Create an operational playbook and intake rubric to evaluate enterprise requests for Dedicated capacity that combine reserved throughput with limited autoscaling/burst (hybrid-reserved). This ticket defines the decision flow for SLA targets, reserved throughput sizing, GPU class selection, burst credit handling, and required preflight tests. Goal is to reduce time-to-offer for sales/solutions and ensure engineering can validate risk and operational cost before commitments.

Scope:
- Intake questionnaire (customer-facing) and internal scoring rubric (cost sensitivity, latency SLA, availability SLA, KV cache locality needs, model variants).
- GPU fit decision matrix that weighs model family, average sequence length, KV cache locality, and requested reserved throughput to recommend GPU class and quantization profile.
- Burst policy & preflight: define burst credit pools, probe jobs for throughput verification, and gating criteria for canary/system tests.
- SLA decomposition: translate customer SLA targets into SLOs/SLIs for routing, autoscale policy, and fallback chains.
- Observability: required dashboards/alerts for acceptance and ongoing SLA monitoring.
- Playbook deliverables: intake form template, triage rubric spreadsheet, sample reply templates for solutions/sales, and a runbook for onboarding after acceptance.
acceptance_criteria: Customer intake form with 15 required fields (workload, model family, p95 latency target, p99 latency target, tokens/sec, burst pattern, KV cache usage, data residency, encryption requirements, expected QPS, growth forecast, zero-downtime windows, allowed GPU families, cost sensitivity band, contact info).
Scoring rubric that outputs a recommended GPU class and reserved throughput (in vGPU/GPU units) and a risk score (low/medium/high).
A decision matrix mapping model characteristics and sequence length distributions to preferred GPU families (A100, H100, L4, etc.) and quantization tiers (fp16, int8/4).
Preflight probe definition and a checklist of tests (throughput probe, latency under cold start, KV cache hit-rate test).
Draft sample SLA decomposition showing how customer p99/p95 maps to routing and autoscale guardrails and fallback model variants.
Confluence page and a triage spreadsheet published and linked in attachments.
tasks: Draft intake form and circulate to Solutions + Sales for feedback (owner: PM)
Define scoring weights and build triage spreadsheet (owner: PM + Data Analyst)
Work with Runtime Eng to codify preflight probes and failure-mode thresholds (owner: Eng lead)
Create recommended GPU fit matrix and upload to Confluence (owner: ML Infra)
Prototype a sample reply template for sales answers (owner: Solutions)
Add dashboard widgets for new SLOs to Console and link in playbook (owner: Observability)
Run two pilot intake evaluations with current customers and record telemetry (owner: Customer Success)
Finalize playbook and schedule a 30-minute training for Solutions/SEs (owner: PM)
updates: 2026-03-10: Ticket created and scoped; initial meeting with Solutions and Runtime Eng scheduled for 2026-03-12.
2026-03-12: Collected feedback from Solutions: emphasized need for 'cost-sensitivity band' and explicit 'KV cache locality' question. Added to intake draft.
2026-03-13: Eng team (Connor Patel) proposed a lightweight probe job (kprobe-v1) for throughput verification; flagged additional infra work if KV cache heat-up > 10s.
2026-03-14: Draft triage spreadsheet updated with scoring weights (latency 35%, throughput 30%, cost tolerance 20%, KV cache reliance 15%). Preparing Confluence page for review.
Next: schedule pilot with Customer A (internal sandbox) and Customer B (opt-in) to validate recommendations and preflight probes.
risks_and_tradeoffs: Conservative GPU recommendations reduce unit cost risk but may underdeliver on p99 for bursty workloads; tradeoff documented in the GPU matrix.
Allowing larger burst credits increases operational complexity and potential for noisy neighbors; recommended to gate bursts to known-safe families (H100/A100) initially.
KV cache heavy workloads require warmup windows and may need 'reserved pins' on pools to guarantee locality — this increases cost and reduces pool utilization.
Automated sizing may produce optimistic reserved throughput for workloads with non-stationary distributions; include explicit growth multiplier in score (default x1.3).
sample_intake_example: Model family: Llama 2-70B (fp16 production), average seq length: 512 tokens
Requested SLA: p95 <= 120ms, p99 <= 450ms; expected steady throughput: 300 tokens/sec, burst up to 5x for 10s every 6 hours
KV cache: heavy (chat history reused across sessions, expect 80% reuse within user session)

Suggested outcome: Recommend reserved throughput 2 x H100 (quantized fp16 preferred), enable limited burst credit on same pool with preflight probes. Risk: KV cache warmup may require 30s of sustained traffic to reach expected hit-rate; propose onboarding warmup window.

source.title: Adaptive alert routing + sticky dedupe for evals regressions triggered by config/model changes
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Problem: Customers and SREs receive noisy eval alerts when model or config changes cause transient drops in eval signals. Current Console alerting can produce duplicates across routes (Slack channels, PagerDuty services) and lacks consistent grouping when the same regression is observed across related eval sets or rollout steps.

Goal: Add an adaptive routing and deduplication layer in Console that ties eval regression alerts to the causative change (model_version, config_hash, rollout_id) and routes a single, grouped alert to the correct on-call or owner with optional fanout. The solution should: (1) reduce noisy duplicates, (2) preserve fast detection and paging for real regressions, (3) be configurable per-team and per-project, and (4) integrate with existing Slack and PagerDuty destinations and our rollout/canary metadata.

Proposed solution:
- Sticky dedupe key: construct a deterministic key for each regression event using a concatenation of [eval_suite_id, signal_group, model_version (if present), config_hash (stable hash of relevant runtime flags), rollout_id (optional)]. This key persists for a sliding dedupe window (default 2 hours) with configurable TTL.
- Adaptive routing rules: allow Console to map dedupe keys to routing endpoints using prioritization rules: e.g., (owner_team -> PagerDuty service), then (workspace channel), then (escalation list). Routing will consider severity (signal drop %), downstream customer exposure (derived from usage keys), and whether the change was internal (deploy) vs. external (model upgrade).
- Grouping & summarized notifications: single message with aggregated metrics (delta over baseline, affected eval cases, example failures, recent commits/PRs) included. Support expansion links back to the Evals dashboard, rollout timeline, and GitHub PR.
- Fallback & fail-open: if routing endpoint is unavailable, fallback to a central alerts channel and create a PagerDuty incident if above severity threshold.
- Telemetry and SLOs: emit events for dedupe-match, dedupe-expire, routed-to-<destination>, and alert-suppressed-by-policy for instrumentation and post-incident analysis. Measure alerts-per-regression, mean time to page, and false positive rate on a weekly dashboard.

Console UX changes:
- New routing configuration page under Project -> Alerts: priority matrix, dedupe window, per-route escalation policy, example dedupe key preview.
- Per-eval-suite settings to opt-in/out and override defaults.
- Alert preview modal with raw dedupe key and sample aggregated payload.

Acceptance criteria:
- Reduce duplicate notifications (same regression) by >= 70% in canary tenants.
- Mean time to page for high-severity regressions <= 5 minutes in canary.
- Provide per-team routing controls in Console and documented API for programmatic rule creation.
- Telemetry events ingested in observability pipeline and a sample dashboard created.

Rollout plan:
1) Implement server-side dedupe store and routing engine behind feature flag (ENG-20451).
2) Expose routing config in Console (small UX).
3) Canary to 10 internal teams, compare before/after duplicate rate and MTTP.
4) Gradual rollout to customers with opt-in, then default enable for high-usage tenants after 4 weeks of stable metrics.

Risks & trade-offs:
- Over-aggressive dedupe could suppress distinct regressions that share a model_version; mitigation: include signal_group and require severity threshold to merge.
- Complexity added to routing rules may confuse users; mitigation: provide sane defaults and an example rule library.

Links & artifacts:
- Confluence spec (draft): https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/EVALS/Adaptive+Alert+Routing+Spec
- Design mockups: https://drive.redwood.ai/drive/u/0/folders/console-alert-routing-mocks
- GitHub PR (engine): https://github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/4821
- GitHub PR (console): https://github.com/redwood-inference/console/pull/3197
- Canary dashboard (Grafana): https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/alerts/evals-dedupe-canary

Operational notes:
- Default dedupe window 2 hours; teams can set 30m - 24h range.
- Dedupe store uses Redis cluster with local fallback; ENG-20451 to prototype TTL semantics.
- PagerDuty mapping will require service metadata; PM to coordinate with SRE and support for on-call import tool.
acceptance_criteria: Console exposes per-project routing config and dedupe ttl
Deduplication reduces duplicate alerts by >=70% in canary tenants
High-severity regressions still page within 5 minutes
Telemetry events (dedupe-match, routed-to-{dest}) available in observability pipeline
Docs and example rules published in Confluence
comments: 2026-03-11 - Marcus: Scoped initial MVP to dedupe + basic PagerDuty/Slack routing. ENG owner confirmed Redis prototype works for dedupe TTL (see ENG-20451).

2026-03-12 - Design feedback (Lina Duarte): Add compact summary layout for Slack so channels aren't flooded with long payloads; include link to 'View details' in Console. Assigned follow-up to DES-432.
2026-03-13 - SRE (Jordan Blake): Recommend limiting dedupe window for low-severity signals to 30m to reduce incident fatigue; added as an adjustable policy option.
2026-03-14 - PM (Ava): Merged engineering and console PRs into feature-flag branch. Started internal canary invite list (10 teams).
Pending: confirm PagerDuty service import flow and whether SCIM/team mapping required.
notes: This ticket differs from previous eval alert work by surfacing the causative change as the primary grouping key and providing adaptive routing rather than simple dedupe-by-signal.
We will link alerts back to rollout metadata so SREs can see whether a regression coincides with a specific release or model update.
Plan to instrument a small sample of customer traffic first to validate false positive impact before broad enablement.

source.title: Private deployment security contracts: SSO/RBAC/KMS/audit acceptance & signoff
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: PRD-level acceptance contract for private (VPC / on-prem) customer onboarding: consolidate interface-level requirements for SSO (SAML/OIDC), RBAC semantics, audit log schema & retention, and KMS/HSM integration. Purpose is to create a deterministic signoff checklist and evidence matrix so Sales/CS/Onboarding engineers can validate compliance needs up front and reduce rework during enterprise onboarding.
requirements_clarifications: SSO: support SAML 2.0 IdP initiated and SP initiated flows; required attributes: email, name, groups; optional attributes mapping documented per-customer in onboarding bundle.
RBAC: roles must support role-mapping from IdP groups and an internal RBAC resource model (org->project->model->route). Document least-privilege personas: Admin, Ops, Billing, Viewer, Model-Deployer.

Audit logs: immutable append-only audit stream with JSON lines output. Each record must include timestamp (ISO8601), actor_id, actor_type, action, target_resource, request_id, outcome, and optional metadata. Retention configurable per-customer (90/365/7 years) and exportable to S3/HTTP/Syslog.

KMS/HSM: support customer-managed keys (CMK) via cloud KMS (AWS/GCP/Azure) with envelope encryption; optional HSM-backed keys via BYOK. Rotation policy: support scheduled rotation and key aliasing to avoid downtime.
Rollout constraints: Private control plane must enforce policy that customers cannot weaken audit retention below contractual minimum for regulated tiers.

Network & evidence: specify expected diagnostic artifacts: SAML metadata XML, IdP test assertion, RBAC mapping CSV, sample audit log export (last 24 hours), KMS key policy JSON, HSM connection test report.
Compatibility: SSO attribute mapping changes must be backward compatible for existing customers; breaking mapping changes require 30-day notice and migration helper script.
Encryption in transit & at rest: TLS 1.2+ enforced for control plane and data plane; at-rest encryption using CMK for model cache snapshots and customer secrets.

Failure modes: clear fallback when KMS unavailable (fail closed vs fail open decision) — default behavior: fail closed for private deployments unless explicit contract allows caching wrapped keys for short-term operations.
Metrics and observability: surface per-tenant audit-forwarding status, key-usage metrics, and SSO auth success/failure rates in Console.
scope_changes: Added explicit HSM BYOK path after security workshop (original PRD only specified KMS).
Narrowed audit export formats to JSON-lines and Syslog only; removed CSV export due to schema mismatch risks.
Deferred per-route fine-grained ABAC policies to a future epic; current scope implements role-based patterns with resource scoping.
Expanded release target window to include additional QA pass for multi-cloud KMS integrations.
Added on-prem installation checklist items: local time sync, CA trust bundle installation, and hardware HSM network requirements.
Minor UI scope reduction: RBAC admin UX will provide CSV import/export but not an advanced visual policy builder in this release.
Clarified that demo tenants used for enterprise sales will have audit retention capped at 30 days by default (configurable in contract).
Introduced mandatory security review and penetration test step prior to customer cutover for higher-regulated tiers.
Performance requirement for audit export pipeline: initial SLA 10s median export latency for 10k events/minute burst during onboarding window.
Decision log: prefer CMK envelope encryption for model keys to avoid exposing raw model weights to customer KMS operations.
All scope changes recorded in onboarding-change-log and require PM+Security+Legal signoff.
Fallback policy: if HSM connection cannot be established during onboarding, we will proceed with cloud KMS for staging and mark HSM provisioning as a blocked dependency for production cutover.
acceptance_criteria: SSO: Successfully authenticate to a staging private deployment using a customer's SAML IdP in both IdP-initiated and SP-initiated flows and evidence assertion logs.
RBAC: Map IdP groups to internal roles and verify that a mapped user cannot access actions outside their assigned role in an isolation test tenant.
Audit logs: Export a 24-hour sample JSON-lines audit file to a test S3 bucket; each record must validate against the audit JSON schema; evidence stored in onboarding artifacts.
KMS: Demonstrate CMK encryption/decryption for stored secrets and model snapshot envelope keys using customer's KMS; include key rotation and alias switch test showing no downtime.
HSM: When customer requests HSM, run HSM connection test and verify key-wrap operations succeed with vendor HSM; record test artifacts and vendor-signed attestation.
Documentation: Produce a per-customer onboarding checklist with required artifacts checklist and a signed acceptance form signed by PM, Security engineer, and Customer SRE.
Security review: Completed threat model and penetration test report for private deployment with critical/major issues addressed or mitigated.
Observability: Console shows SSO health, key usage, and audit-forwarding status for the tenant; alerts fire on export failures to customer sink.
Legal/compliance: Customer contract clause for minimum audit retention is captured in the onboarding agreement and referenced in the acceptance artifact bundle.
Operational runbook: On-call runbook for KMS/HSM failures and audit export incidents is published and validated in a tabletop exercise.
Rollback & cutover: Cutover run executed in staging, with rollback taken and verified in under 60 minutes per agreed plan.
Access review: A quarterly access review process is documented and a test review completed during onboarding with findings remediated.
Performance: Audit export pipeline meets the performance requirement defined in scope changes under a synthetic load test.
Privacy: PII minimization confirmed for audit log fields and a redaction policy documented for customer-specific sensitive metadata.
All acceptance artifacts uploaded to the onboarding evidence folder (links below) and the signoff checklist ticked.
Signoffs captured from stakeholders: PM, Security, Legal, Onboarding Engineer, Customer Security Representative.
signoff_checklist: PM: Confirm scope & deliverables, attach onboarding checklist and ensure commercial terms reflect retention choices.
Security Engineer: Validate threat model, review audit schema, confirm KMS/HSM architecture and approve pen test remediation.
Legal: Confirm contract language for audit retention, BYOK/HSM responsibilities, and liability clauses.
Onboarding Engineer: Provide evidence artifacts (SAML metadata, sample audit export, KMS policy JSON, HSM attestation) and run verification checklist.
Customer Security Rep: Provide signed acceptance for SAML mapping, RBAC role definitions, and confirm retention policy.
Support/OPS: Confirm monitoring/alerting present and runbook published; verify that Console surfacing of SSO/KMS/audit metrics is active.
Sales/Account Exec: Confirm commercial acceptance criteria met, including any custom SLAs and pricing adjustments for HSM.
Compliance/External Auditor (if required): Confirm evidence package meets regulatory checklists (e.g., SOC2/ISO/GDPR as applicable).
Release Manager: Verify release train includes the private deployment changes and schedule a gated cutover window.
PM signoff: final acceptance form filed and issue moved to Done.
Security review flag: set to true and reference security_review_ticket if further follow-ups are required.
Post-cutover review scheduled within 14 days to capture learnings and update onboarding templates.
All artifacts uploaded to: DRIVE_LINK_PLACEHOLDER, CONFLUENCE_DOC_PLACEHOLDER, GH_PR_PLACEHOLDER, and monitoring runbook stored in OPS_RUNBOOK_LINK.
Evidence bundle must include cryptographic proofs for key alias swap and audit export checksum (SHA256) for verification.
linked_artifacts: CONFLUENCE: Private Deploy Security Contracts (draft) - CONFLUENCE_DOC_PLACEHOLDER
risks: KMS multi-cloud nuances cause unexpected key-usage errors during rotation; mitigation: extra QA and vendor matrix with known limitations.
SAML attribute mismatch causing partial access; mitigation: conservative defaults and mapping preview tool before cutover.
HSM vendor network requirements delay provisioning for on-prem customers; mitigation: pre-onboarding checklist and SLA with vendor.
Audit export throughput spikes could overwhelm customer sinks; mitigation: backpressure and configurable batching with throttling alerts.
Legal/regulatory variance across regions may require per-customer contract text; mitigation: involve Legal early and provide templated clauses.
Operational complexity increases onboarding time; mitigation: create an onboarding playbook and automation for artifact validation.
If fail-closed KMS policy triggers during business hours, customer operations could be blocked; mitigation: implementation of an emergency unlock process with two-person auth.
Data residency specifics not captured in early requirements; mitigation: include residency checklist in initial scoping call.
implementation_notes: Use feature-flag gated rollout for HSM paths to allow progressive enablement after vendor validation.
Audit schema stored as JSON Schema in the repo and validated by CI on every change.
Provide a small 'onboarding helper' CLI that validates SAML metadata, runs an example assertion, and performs audit export sanity checks.
Create a KMS compatibility matrix (AWS/GCP/Azure) and include known limitations in the onboarding docs.
Ensure Console surfaces a staged guidance banner for tenants in onboarding to avoid confusion during feature rollout.
Store evidence artifacts in a locked Drive folder with access controls and retention rules aligned to contract.
Implement an audit export checksum (SHA256) and signed manifest to provide non-repudiable evidence for customers.
Coordinate with Platform infra to allocate a staging private control plane for each enterprise onboarding test.
Build E2E tests for SAML+RBAC+audit path and schedule nightly runs against a matrix of IdP assertions.
Create a templated acceptance form PDF that auto-populates with checklist items and signer fields for quick signoff.
next_steps: Complete RFC for HSM BYOK integration and secure vendor list by 2026-03-20.
Assign ENG owner for audit export performance optimizations and create ENG ticket (ENG-6123).
Schedule tabletop run with Onboarding, Security, and Support teams on 2026-03-22.
Produce final onboarding checklist and attach to this ticket; notify Sales to use the new bundle for active deals.
Book legal review slot and update contract templates with retention and liability clauses by 2026-03-18.
Run an internal dry-cutover in staging using a mock customer and submit acceptance artifacts to drive link above.
Close loop with infra to provision per-tenant staging environments and confirm runbook operations.
After signoff, create post-cutover retrospective ticket and capture lessons learned within 14 days.

source.title: Progressive upgrade orchestration: customer migration roadmap and adaptive comms kit
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Purpose:
This ticket defines a reusable, progressive customer upgrade playbook and a set of adaptive communication templates tailored to Redwood's LTS / stable channel strategy. The goal is to give account teams and automated tooling a deterministic path for migrating customers across minor/major versions with clear rollout gates, measurable canary success criteria, and templated external comms (email, console banner, changelog, migration guide).

Scope and non-goals:
- In scope: customer-facing artifacts (changelogs, targeted migration emails, staged console banners), gating criteria for automatic/assisted upgrades, SDK compatibility mapping, example rollback messaging, and links to implementation tasks for automation.
- Out of scope: runtime binary build engineering, CI job creation (ENG will own), and legal acceptance for contract changes.

Key principles:
1) Minimize customer surprise: every change that can affect API shape, performance, or billing must have an explicit migration note and migration testing steps.
2) Offer tiers of upgrade experience: automatic (low-risk patch), assisted (major with opt-in), and manual (breaking changes).
3) Gate on observable health: define concrete metrics and thresholds for canary success before expanding rollout.
4) Provide accountable owners and SLAs for reply windows on migration notices.

Deliverables (customer-facing):
- "Progressive Migration Roadmap" one-pager that maps release types to upgrade method (auto/assist/manual) and expected timelines.
- Customer-facing changelog template with structured sections: Summary, Impact (API/behavior/billing), Action required, Migration steps, Rollback instructions, Links to SDK/PRs, Contact & support.
- Three comms templates: Early Heads-up (30d), Upgrade Notice (14d), Final Switch Reminder (72h). Each with subject lines, short preview text, and console banner copy.
- Migration guide skeleton per impact class (no-change, opt-in improvements, breaking-change) with concrete example code diffs for SDKs (Python/Node).
- Canary and rollout gate definitions: SLO/metric thresholds, sample dashboards, and a runbook for rollback.

Rollout gates (proposed):
- Canary cohort: 0.5% of active requests (or 5 low-volume customer tenants) for 48 hours.
- Health checks during canary: 99th percentile latency within +15% of baseline, error rate <= baseline + 0.1% absolute, downstream queue lengths not growing.
- Quality checks: regression tests on canonical prompt set pass (>=98% similarity score), embedding stability within tolerance thresholds.
- Business checks: top 10 paying customers in cohort have no critical issues reported for 72 hours.

Escalation & rollback triggers:
- Immediate rollback if: spike in 5xx errors > 0.5% absolute vs baseline, latency p95 increase > 50% sustained for 10 minutes, or failing critical regression tests.
- Escalation path: on-call SRE -> Release Lead -> PM -> Customer Success for impacted accounts.

Channels and cadence for comms:
- 30 days before: Heads-up email to owners and console banner for customers on LTS/stable channel.
- 14 days before: Formal Upgrade Notice with migration checklist and link to trial window (staging).
- 7 days before: Optional webinar/AM session invite for higher-touch customers.
- 72 hours before: Final reminder + automatic scheduling window for Assisted Upgrades (if applicable).
- Day-of: Banner change + in-console migration helper (one-click where supported).

Automation and toolkit items (ENG deliverables):
- Endpoint for in-console upgrade scheduling + status webhooks (dependency ENG-7329).
- CLI tool extension to perform preflight checks and produce a migration report.
- SDK compatibility matrix generator (map runtime-1.20 to SDK versions).

Ownership and responsibilities:
- PM: draft templates, coordinate approvals, sign-off on gate definitions (Marcus Patel).
- Release Engineering: implement automation hooks and canary routing (ENG team).
- DevRel/Docs: publish migration guide and code diffs.
- Customer Success: staging outreach and high-touch migrations.
- Legal/Security: review only if new data handling introduced.

Telemetry & dashboards (links):
- Canary dashboard: https://dashboard.redwood.ai/releases/runtime-1.20/canary (placeholder)
- Regression test report: https://ci.redwood.ai/pipelines/runtime-1.20/regressions
- Migration rollout tracker: https://console.redwood.ai/releases/17105/overview

Acceptance criteria (for PM sign-off):
- Templates approved by Marketing & CS and loaded into the comms library.
- Runbook with rollback triggers published and tested in a canary.
- A sample assisted upgrade performed for 3 pilot customers without critical regressions.
- SDK compatibility table published and linked from the migration guide.
comments: 2026-03-02 - Marcus Patel: Kickoff sync with ENG and CS. Agreed to target runtime-1.20 as first LTS candidate. Assigned automation tasks to ENG-7329.

2026-03-05 - Elena Gomez (Docs): Drafted migration guide skeleton and example Python diff for tokenized input change. Shared in doc: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/RL/PM-17105-migration-skeleton
2026-03-09 - Release Lead (Tom Hughes): Proposed canary cohort size lowered to 0.5% for better risk containment; added explicit p95 latency gate.
2026-03-12 - Marcus Patel: Reviewed email templates with CS. Need to add opt-out clause for enterprise customers; action: CS to prepare list of opt-outs by 2026-03-20.
2026-03-14 - QA: Regression harness added to nightly pipeline; initial canonical prompt set run shows 99.1% similarity - meets threshold.
templates:
HEADS-UP (30 days):
Subject: Upcoming runtime improvements (runtime-1.20) and what it means for your workload
Preview: We’ll be rolling out an LTS update with performance and stability improvements. No immediate action required for most customers; opt-in steps available for advanced changes.
Body: Hello {{owner_name}},
We’re planning to roll out runtime-1.20 to the LTS channel in ~30 days. This update focuses on CPU/GPU kernel improvements and a minor API stabilization that could affect custom caching logic. Summary of impact: none for most customers; action required only if you use custom KV caching APIs. Full migration guide: <link>. If you’d like a staging window, schedule here: <link>.
-- The Redwood Inference Team

UPGRADE NOTICE (14 days):
Subject: Action required: scheduled LTS upgrade to runtime-1.20 (14 days)
Preview: Please review the migration checklist and confirm your preferred upgrade path.
Body: Hello {{owner_name}},
As a follow-up, your tenant is slated for the LTS upgrade on {{scheduled_date}}. Please review the attached migration checklist and run the preflight CLI (link) to validate your environment. If you require assistance, reply to this message or open a support ticket.
-- CS & Release

FINAL REMINDER (72h):
Subject: Reminder: runtime-1.20 upgrade in 72 hours - last chance to opt out
Preview: The upgrade will occur during your maintenance window unless you request an exception.
Body: Hello {{owner_name}},
This is a final reminder for your scheduled LTS upgrade. If you need to opt out or reschedule, contact your account team within 48 hours. After the upgrade, rollback windows are limited; we recommend running the migration preflight.
-- Redwood Support

source.title: QBR Board Prep: Technical Risk Heatmap + Investment Asks
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Purpose: Prepare a concise technical-risk heatmap and associated investment asks for the upcoming quarterly business review (QBR) and board packet. Goal is to surface the top 6 technical risks that could materially affect our Q2 SLAs, capacity commitments for Dedicated customers, and Private deployment stability, and to recommend budget/CAPEX or headcount asks with clear decision criteria.

Scope: Analysis will cover serving runtime risks (batching/KV cache regressions), capacity limits and burst behavior for Dedicated, Private upgrade/rollback risk, operational observability gaps that increase MTTR, and model-compatibility / kernel support gaps that could force fallbacks. Excludes product roadmap feature asks not directly tied to risk reduction.

Deliverables: (1) A one-page heatmap slide (likelihood vs impact) with 6 prioritized risks and mitigations; (2) Two-slide investment ask deck (ask, expected ROI / risk reduction, timeline, success criteria); (3) Appendix with data sources (SLO trend graphs, incident summaries, capacity forecast table, cost delta models).

Data sources: SLO dashboards (Datadog links), incident postmortems (Confluence), capacity forecast model (Google Sheet), Dedicated tenant performance sampling (internal API), kernel/quantization backlog (GitHub labels).

Decision points for execs/board: Approve a $600k Q2 mitigation reserve for Dedicated burst capacity and cross-region fallbacks; approve hiring 1 full-time runtime engineer for kernel/quantization work; prioritize shipping KV-cache determinism improvements in runtime-1.18 vs deferring to runtime-1.19.

Success criteria post-QBR: Exec sign-off on asks or explicit deferral with tradeoffs documented; updated roadmap items and owners; measurable reduction in projected P50/P95 latency tail risk across Dedicated forecast for next quarter.
next_steps: Finish heatmap draft slide (Rafael) - due 2026-03-15
Run capacity delta scenarios with finance and infra (Maya) - due 2026-03-16
Pull incident summaries for last 6 months and extract root causes (SRE) - due 2026-03-14
Draft investment ask language and ROI table (Ava) - due 2026-03-17
Schedule 30m review with CTO and Head of Sales (PM ops) - due 2026-03-18
acceptance_criteria: One-page heatmap slide approved by CTO and Head of Product
Investment ask slide shows clear ROI or failure-to-act cost estimates
Appendix includes SLO trend and at least 3 incident rollups supporting each high-risk item
Owners and next steps recorded in Roadmap & OKRs 2026-Q2 doc

2026-03-11 Rafael Ortiz: Pulled capacity forecast and flagged a 22% worst-case burst shortfall for Dedicated in ap-southeast region. Added to high-risk column.

2026-03-12 Priya Patel (SRE): Incident extraction in progress. Found two regressions tied to KV cache invalidation affecting tail latency during multi-tenant GC windows.
2026-03-13 CTO (review): Suggested framing asks as 'mitigation reserve + runway for kernel hire'. Requested concrete success metrics for 90-day and 180-day horizons.
2026-03-13 Ava Chen: Incorporated CTO feedback and updated decision points. Rafael to prepare heatmap visualization and Maya to finalize ROI table.
2026-03-13 Rafael Ortiz: Uploaded preliminary slide to Confluence; shared link in attachments. Need SRE incident excerpts and Datadog panels embedded before review.
risks: If investment is declined, Dedicated may face increased tail latency and potential SLA credits in Q2 for top-tier customers
Delay in kernel/quantization hires increases risk of needing more expensive hosted fallbacks into H2
Incomplete SLO evidence may weaken the board ask; must ensure incident rollups are concise and attributable

Approve hiring 1 senior runtime engineer (kernel/quantization) with urgency 30-60 days
Prioritize KV-cache determinism work into runtime-1.18 milestone, or explicitly accept risk of deferring to 1.19
follow_up_tasks: Add approved asks to finance request tracker (Maya)
Create hiring req and start candidate pipeline (People Ops + Eng Hiring)
Schedule post-QBR 1-hour technical deep-dive for board members wanting details

2026-03-13 CTO feedback recorded and ticket updated by Ava Chen

Decision points for execs/board: Approve a $600k Q2 mitigation reserve for Dedicated burst capacity and cross-region fallbacks; approve hiring 1 full-time runtime engineer for kernel/quantization work; prioritize shipping KV-cache determinism improvements in runtime-1.18 vs deferring to runtime-1.19.

Success criteria post-QBR: Exec sign-off on asks or explicit deferral with tradeoffs documented; updated roadmap items and owners; measurable reduction in projected P50/P95 latency tail risk across Dedicated forecast for next quarter.

source.title: MSA QuickPass: Automated low-risk redline approvals with pricing delta flagging
source.creator: Priya Nair
source.description: Build an automated QuickPass workflow for Master Service Agreement (MSA) and accompanying DPA redlines that allows low-risk requests to be approved without full legal review, while surfacing a pricing delta and audit trail to Sales and Finance. The flow should integrate security questionnaire scores, indemnity/liability caps thresholds, and a simple UI in the quote screen that shows the required price uplift if the customer requests concessions outside the QuickPass envelope. This reduces cycle time for low-risk customers and lets us run pricing/packaging experiments to measure revenue impact of accepting small liability increases vs. rejecting or escalating.
acceptance_criteria: QuickPass decision engine evaluates redline request in < 5s and returns: auto-approve | escalate | reject
Decision rules reference: indemnity cap <= $250k, liability cap <= $500k or relative to ARR <= 2x, and security questionnaire score >= 85 -> eligible for auto-approve
Quote UI shows a computed pricing delta and reason code when QuickPass would auto-approve with surcharge (example: +8% for indemnity uplift)
Audit trail persisted to customer record: redline text snapshot, decision inputs, reviewer (if any), PR/GR IDs, and telemetry events
End-to-end e2e tests for rule variants, and a canary run for 10% of small-business deals for 2 weeks with telemetry capture
Legal sign-off on rule thresholds and wording for the QuickPass approved MSA amendment template
comments: 2026-03-10 Priya Nair: Initial scope write-up after discovery with Sales and Legal; goal to reduce median contract turnaround for SMB/SME by 40%.

2026-03-11 Legal (M. Alvarez): Reviewed proposed thresholds; suggested changing indemnity absolute cap to be a hybrid (min(abs) + % of ARR). Asked to include an explicit fallback that escalates to Legal Ops for any export-control or data residency clauses.
2026-03-12 Design (A. Chen): Uploaded quick mock for quote UI (Drive link below). Mock includes compact badge, reason tooltip, and 'Apply QuickPass surcharge' toggle for Sales.

2026-03-12 Engineering (E. Park): Feasibility note — decision engine implemented as a ruleset service; will reuse existing rule-eval infra from ENG-1842. Need a small schema change to quote event payload to include ARR and security_score.

2026-03-13 PM: Decided to run as a pricing experiment with a control cohort; product measurement to track acceptance rate, lift in deal velocity, average price uplift, and any downstream support/legal churn.
2026-03-13 Legal Ops: Requested audit log retention 7 years for enterprises accepting indemnity uplift and flagged security_review_required = true for those cases.
decision_log: Use hybrid indemnity threshold: min($250k) OR 2x ARR (whichever is lower) as primary auto-approve threshold — Legal approved 2026-03-11
Price uplift cadence: round to nearest 1% and cap uplift at 15% for QuickPass approvals to avoid extreme surcharges — decision 2026-03-12
Escalation triggers: security_score < 85, non-standard data residency, or any clause edits to sections S3/S4 -> immediate Legal Ops handoff
audit_and_telemetry: Event: contract.quickpass.requested (payload: customer_id, deal_id, ARR, security_score, redline_digest)
Event: contract.quickpass.decision (payload: decision, rule_ids, pricing_delta_pct, timestamp)
Persist: redline snapshot to secure storage path: s3://redwood-contracts/audit/<deal_id>/snapshot.json
Daily report: count auto-approved vs escalated vs rejected by cohort (control/experiment)
risk_and_mitigation: Risk: Overly permissive rules could increase indemnity exposure. Mitigation: conservative initial thresholds and manual weekly review by Legal Ops for first 4 weeks.
Risk: Sales may hide customer ARR or gamesmanship. Mitigation: pull ARR from billing source of truth and mark quotes where ARR is estimated; estimated ARR forces escalation.
Risk: Price uplift harms conversion. Mitigation: A/B test control cohort without QuickPass to measure true impact and monitor win-rate by segment
rollout_plan: Phase 0 (pilot): Internal Sales + 5 strategic SMB accounts, 1 week
Phase 1 (canary): 10% of SMB cohort with telemetry gating (2 weeks)
Phase 2: 50% of SMB and selective mid-market with stricter thresholds (4 weeks)
Phase 3: Full rollout for eligible segments after sign-off

source.title: Model Bundling Economics & Regional Substitution Simulations
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Objective: Determine optimal productized bundles and procurement trigger rules that prioritize latency-sensitive customers while minimizing expected unit cost across regions. We will synthesize telemetry and market pricing to simulate realistic substitution events (model provider swaps, quantization fallbacks, regional outages) and produce a recommended tiered bundling strategy (latency-first, cost-first, balanced) plus procurement escalation criteria for Dedicated/Private sales.

Scope:
- Competitive scan of bundled offers (managed model bundles, reserved GPU + hosting credits, enterprise quant bundles) across 6 vendors in NA/EU/APAC.
- Build Monte Carlo substitution simulator that ingests: per-model token cost, per-region median/95th latency distributions, quantization quality penalties (per workload class), auto-batching impact curves, and SLA rollback probabilities.
- Run simulations for 3 buyer archetypes: latency-first (customer-facing RAG), throughput-first (batch embeddings), and mixed (chat + search).
- Deliverables: spreadsheet of decision boundaries, set of procurement trigger rules (e.g., fallback when 95th-latency > X ms and unit-cost delta > Y%), PRD with recommended bundle SKUs and pricing fence, and a short executive slide deck.

Hypotheses:
1) A small number (2-3) of latency-first bundles with explicit regional fallbacks will retain >95% of revenue under common outage scenarios while reducing expected token-cost by ~12% vs unconstrained hosted pricing.
2) Enterprise procurement teams will accept regional substitution if contractual SLOs and deterministic failover paths are explicit in the SKU.

Key success metrics:
- Simulator: produces calibrated cost/latency distributions matching console telemetry within 10%.
- Business: bundle recommendations projected to improve unit economics for 60% of target accounts without increasing 95th-latency beyond customer tolerance.

Assumptions:
- We can access per-model per-region latency and cost telemetry (Console traces + billing).
- Quantization quality penalties can be approximated from prior inference-eval work and applied as multiplicative F1/QA deltas.
comments: 2026-03-01 — Triage: flagged as priority for pricing committee. Initial outline approved by product leadership (A. Chen).
2026-03-03 — Data request submitted to console-infra: full day-level latency histograms by region and model. Ticket: ENG-20021. Marcus asked for 90/95/99 pcts plus median and sample size per hour.

2026-03-05 — Competitive scan in progress. Found two vendors packaging reserved capacity with RAG-focused SLAs; pricing buckets differ by endpoint and by region. Draft notes saved to Confluence: https://confluence.redwood.company/display/PRIC/Model+Bundling+Competitive+Scan

2026-03-06 — PM alignment: legal asked for draft contractual language covering region-based substitution and telemetry disclosure. Ran by Legal (S. Patel). Action: include explicit SLA rollback language in PRD.

2026-03-08 — Engineering sync: agreed on simulator inputs and output schema. ENG to provide an anonymized sample trace and cost mapping by 2026-03-11. Prototype repo: https://github.com/redwood-inference/model-bundling-sim

2026-03-10 — Early simulation run (n=10k trials) shows latency-first bundle maintains <200ms median but 95th latency spikes in APAC when a preferred provider is unavailable. Marcus: add multi-provider fallback rules and quantify failover cost.
2026-03-12 — Design note: created initial SKU naming convention and slide mockups. Design owner: Priya Rao. Link to draft deck: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1RBwExample
Next steps: finalize data ingestion (console traces + billing mapping), run full Monte Carlo (100k trials) per buyer archetype, draft PRD for pricing working group by 2026-03-26.

source.title: Sprint-to-strategy translation kit and exec briefing playbook
source.creator: Nora Martinez
source.description: Purpose: Create a repeatable 'translation kit' and playbook that converts sprint-level metrics, qualitative signals, and open risks into a concise executive narrative for monthly executive readouts, QBRs, and board packets. Background: PMs and Eng leads currently handcraft slides for each readout; this is time-consuming and inconsistently framed which makes cross-initiative comparison hard. Goal: reduce time to prepare an exec-ready one-pager from ~8 hours to <2 hours and to standardize signals used to drive OKR updates and investment asks.
deliverables: One-pager template + fillable data sections (metrics, health, runway, top risks)
Deck starter slides (3 variants: ops, product, finance-aligned) and speaker notes
Automated data pull configs for Grafana -> CSV and a script to populate the one-pager
Decision log template (playbackable) and CI to append finalized decisions
Runbook for escalation lanes triggered during readouts
Confluence how-to with examples and checklist
acceptance_criteria: Template renders populated one-pager from sprint exports without manual editing in 90% of cases
Execs report readout clarity >= 4/5 in the post-readout survey for two consecutive months
PMs can generate initial deck in <2 hours using the kit
All data sources required are documented and automated pulls are failing <5% on readout days
risks: Telemetry gaps for new initiatives may require manual overrides
Automated pulls rely on dashboard naming conventions that are not yet enforced
Executive preference variance – some execs prefer narrative vs metrics-heavy slides

source.title: Hybrid-Minute Reservation Bands, Soft-Preempt Policy, and SLA Tier Pilot
source.creator: Aisha Patel
source.description:
Context: Enterprise customers request more granular reserved capacity options to better match variable workloads (short bursts and minute-level spikes) without paying for long-duration commitments. Current committed-hour SKU is too coarse for many ML infra patterns (jobs with 1-30 minute peaks).

Hypothesis: Introducing minute-level reservation bands (e.g., 15, 60, 180 minute commit bands) combined with a soft-preempt policy and a small overage credit pool will increase conversion from trial to committed customers by 8-12% among mid-market accounts and reduce short-term overprovision requests, while preserving predictable revenue.

Proposal: Run a 12-week pilot offering a new SKU family: Hybrid-Minute. Key elements:
- Reservation bands sold as bundles of guaranteed-minutes per month (e.g., 10k x 15m blocks, 4k x 60m blocks).
- Soft-preempt policy: when a reserved minute is consumed, the system will throttle non-reserved jobs within same tenant before preempting reserved slots; preemption only occurs when contention persists >60s.
- Overprovision protection credits: each subscription includes a small pool of 'grace credits' that cover minute-level overages at a reduced rate and are replenished monthly.
- SLA tiers: Bronze (best-effort), Silver (99.5 latency SLO for <2s p95), Gold (99.9 latency SLO + 1hr incident response SLA).
- Admin console controls: visibility into minute-budget consumption, one-click fine-tune to convert spent min-bands into short-term burst buys.

Objectives:
- Reduce ad-hoc burst requests to support and sales by 30% for pilot customers.
- Demonstrate unit economics at scale (target per-minute cost within 25% of existing per-hour reserved SKU).

Scope: Pricing model, runtime preemption & throttle rules, billing & metering changes, console UI, legal addendum for SLA tiers, and enterprise security questionnaire updates. Excluded: Dedicated physical machine redesign or new hardware procurement.
acceptance_criteria: Pilot SKU implemented in billing and metering with clear minute aggregation in usage logs
Runtime implements soft-preempt policy behind feature flag and can be toggled per-organization
Console shows minute-band consumption and forecast with at least 7-day forecast accuracy (MAPE < 20%)
Legal has an SLA addendum template for Bronze/Silver/Gold signed off by security and revenue ops
At least 5 pilot customers onboarded and billing exercised with >90% telemetry coverage
success_metrics: Conversion uplift: +8-12% of pilot customers convert to any reserved product within 90 days
Support volume: 30% reduction in burst/overage sales requests from pilot customers
Revenue: pilot margin at or above 75% of comparable hourly-reserved SKU per-minute basis
SLO adherence: Silver and Gold SLOs met for >= 99.5% of minutes during pilot
decisions: Use minute-level aggregation for billing (not seconds) to limit billing churn
Implement soft-preempt (throttling non-reserved queues first) rather than hard eviction to reduce user impact
Offer 3 minute-band sizes (15m, 60m, 180m) to balance complexity and flexibility
Price nuance: grace credits priced at 60% of on-demand per-minute equivalent to incentivize reservation purchase
tradeoffs: Operational complexity: increased metering and UI complexity vs. customer flexibility and better fit
Revenue predictability: more variable than fixed-hour commitments but expected to capture incremental customers who would otherwise remain on on-demand
Runtime complexity: soft-preempt adds scheduling overhead and state tracking; mitigated by feature flags and canary rollout
SLA tiers: higher operational cost for Gold (response + dedicated runbook) but enables higher ASP for large contracts
implementation_notes: Billing: extend existing usage ingestion to support minute buckets, preserve existing hourly SKU for backward compatibility
Runtime: add per-tenant minuteBudget tracker and throttle policy with metrics exposed via tracing
Console: new 'Minute Bands' panel under Dedicated SKU page, estimate impact of converting bands to burst buys
Legal/SQ: prepare an addendum and updated security questionnaire responses for minute-level metering and preempt behaviors
Telemetry: emit 'minute_band_consumed' event with context (org, model, region, SKU) for every minute consumed
comments: 2026-02-20 Aisha Patel: Drafted proposal after sales calls with 12 mid-market accounts that cited short duty-cycle workloads. Added hypothesis and objectives.

2026-02-24 Marcus Lin (ENG): Reviewed runtime implications. Suggest feature-flagging preempt logic and adding a 60s grace window to avoid flapping. Estimated 3 sprint effort for core scheduler changes.
2026-02-28 Legal (T. Moreno): Need to add explicit customer disclosure about soft-preempt behavior and failure modes in SQ responses. Will draft addendum template.
2026-03-03 Revenue Ops (S. Gupta): Modeling shows pilot can be cash-flow neutral if price per-minute is 55-70% of equivalent hourly rate; recommending initial pricing at 60% to test elasticity.
2026-03-06 Design (L. Ortega): Console mocks attached to Confluence. UX feedback: surface predicted savings vs hourly-reserved and highlight 'convert to burst' CTA where usage spikes often.

2026-03-12 Marcus Lin (ENG): Runtime feature flagged in staging, smoke tests pass for minute aggregation. Billing schema PR opened (see links). Ready to begin onboarding first customer cohort next week.

source.title: Stratified prompt sampling and actionable regression contracts for Optimize evals
source.creator: Aisha Khatri
source.description: Define a reproducible, actionable evaluation contract for Optimize that ties prompt-set sampling, regression thresholds, and A/B decision rules to concrete rollout/rollback actions. The objective is to make eval outputs prescriptive (what to do) rather than descriptive (what happened). This ticket consolidates requirements for sampling stratification, evidence thresholds, stop/rollback logic, monitoring signals, and handoff to SRE/eng for automations.
source.background: Current Optimize evals surface many signals (BLEU-like similarity, human-labeled correctness, hallucination counts, latency/cost per token) but lack a single playbook that maps observed regressions to deterministic actions. Teams have inconsistent sample stratification and different tolerance for small regressions in long-tail prompts. This leads to confusion during rollouts and high false-positive rollback behavior. We need a repeatable contract that is clear to product, engineering, and SRE.
scope: In scope: design of prompt-set stratification schema, per-metric threshold definitions (absolute and relative), A/B test stopping rules and thresholds, canary gating logic and automated rollback conditions, monitoring/alert specs for console dashboards.
Out of scope: infra implementation of evaluation runners (ENG infra ticket will follow), UI polish on console dashboards beyond required widgets, long-term ML model retraining strategy.
acceptance_criteria: A1: A stratified prompt sampling schema is documented with explicit bucket keys (domain, difficulty, prompt-length, zero-shot vs few-shot, sensitive-content flag) and sampling ratios for each rollout tier (smoke, canary, ramp).

A2: For each primary metric (semantic similarity, correctness, hallucination rate, latency p50/p95, tokens-per-request cost) provide: baseline definition, statistical test, minimum sample size, absolute and relative thresholds that trigger a fail state.

A3: A/B test decision rules are defined with Type I/II controls (alpha=0.01 for high-risk releases, alpha=0.05 default), minimum detectable effect (MDE) guidelines per metric, and sequential testing rules that prevent peeking bias.

A4: Rollback policy maps signal combinations to actions: immediate auto-rollback (critical safety regressions), staged rollback (quality drop + cost spike), manual review required (small regression on non-safety metric). The mapping is documented and signed off by PM, SRE, and Security.
A5: Monitoring and alerts spec is provided with concrete alert thresholds for console and an incident runbook skeleton linked.
A6: Test plan with synthetic and production-sampled prompt sets and an executable checklist for a canary rollout is documented and validated by the Solutions Eng team in a dry run.
evaluation_design: Stratification keys: domain (search/chat/summarization/code), difficulty (easy/medium/hard based on prior error rate), prompt length bucket (<=64, 65-256, 257+), prompt shape (instruction-only vs multi-turn), sensitive-content flag (PII/medical/legal), representative customer segments.

Sampling ratios: smoke=100 random global prompts; canary=2k prompts sampled stratified: domain: 40% chat, 25% search, 20% summarization, 15% code; difficulty: 50% medium, 30% hard, 20% easy; ensure minimum 50 prompts per (domain x difficulty) cell.

Metrics to compute per-prompt: semantic-similarity (embedding cosine vs baseline), binary-correctness (human or automated rubric), hallucination-rate (hallucination flags per 100 tokens), latency p50/p95, cost-per-request (tokens * model-cost-per-token).

Baseline windows: rolling 14-day baseline for hosted models; stable pinned-version baseline for Dedicated/Private releases. All baselines must include at least 5k prior prompts unless sample-scarce customers are being evaluated (document exemption).

Sample-size guidance: use two-sample proportions for binary metrics (power=0.8 default). Provide lookup table: for MDE=3% require ~2,500 samples per arm at alpha=0.05 for binary correctness; increase sample if stratified analysis is required.

Signal aggregation: measure both global average and per-bucket deltas; require that no single high-weighted bucket exceeds its bucket-threshold and that the weighted composite delta exceeds the composite threshold to trigger action.
ab_test_spec: Default hypothesis framework: H0 = no difference vs H1 = new model improves/worsens metric by >= MDE. Specify one-sided tests for targeted improvements, two-sided for safety regressions.

Statistical controls: default alpha=0.05, beta=0.2; for safety/security-sensitive rollouts use alpha=0.01 and require human approval for any passing signal. Use Bonferroni correction if testing >5 independent primary metrics or use Benjamini-Hochberg for FDR control for many secondary metrics.

Sequential testing: adopt group-sequential design with O'Brien-Fleming boundaries for interim looks. Minimum effective sample fraction per look = 25% with pre-specified number of looks <= 4. Enforce alpha spending schedule via the test harness in Optimize.

Decision rules: pass if primary metric(s) show improvement that is statistically significant and no critical safety metric regresses beyond absolute threshold. If multiple primaries, require a weighted quorum (e.g., at least 2 of 3 primaries) unless an outright safety breach occurs.
Tie-breaker: for ambiguous results within MDE margin, require qualitative review on a stratified 200-sample human-labeled set and escalate to PM/ML lead.
rollback_triggers: Immediate auto-rollback: any regression where hallucination-rate increases by >= 2x absolute and absolute hallucination rate > 1% on safety-sensitive buckets, OR a confirmed PII leakage incident detected by the detection pipeline.

Staged auto-rollback: composite score = 0.6*quality_delta_norm + 0.3*latency_delta_norm + 0.1*cost_delta_norm. If composite score > 0.8 (degradation) for 30m sustained during canary with sample coverage >=50% of strat buckets => auto-rollback.
Manual review required: global semantic-similarity drop >=3% but <6% (relative) triggers investigation and pause of rollout until triage completes within 24h. If not remediated, escalate to rollback.

Cooldown and reattempts: after auto-rollback, block re-rollout of same model variant for 72h unless a patch/mitigation is applied and verified in a fresh canary. Document required artifacts for re-rollout (root-cause analysis, targeted fixes, re-eval results).
monitoring_signals: Dashboards: per-route and per-model panes showing primary metrics by bucket (correctness, semantic-similarity), latency p50/p95, cost-per-request, traffic fraction covered by eval sampling.

Alerts: severity P0 alert on immediate auto-rollback conditions; P1 alert for composite score crossing 0.6; P2 warning for small, noncritical deltas. Alerts must include top 5 offending buckets and links to sampled prompt examples.
Data freshness: evaluation pipeline must surface sample timestamps; dashboards must indicate staleness > 2h. If staleness > 6h during a rollout, pause rollout automatically and notify SRE.
notes_and_decisions: Decision: use weighted composite score for staged actions to balance quality vs latency vs cost (approved by PM and Eng leads 2026-03-11).
Decision: default alpha=0.05 but enforce alpha=0.01 for any rollout that touches enterprise Dedicated customers (compliance sensitivity).
Trade-off: tighter thresholds reduce false negatives but increase operational overhead; selected thresholds aim for <5% false positives in historical backtests on 6 months of past rollouts.
open_questions: Q1: Should we include an automated semantic-rollback when embedding-similarity drops but human-correctness does not? (Need ML input on correlation).
Q2: Are there legal/compliance constraints on retaining sampled prompts for manual review in Private deployments? Security/legal to confirm retention policies.
Q3: Do we want to expose bucket-level thresholds to customers in Dedicated/Private or keep them internal?
test_plan: Dry run: run the full canary checklist against staging with two model variants and verify alerting and auto-rollback behave as expected (owner: Solutions Eng).
Backtests: run the composite scoring algorithm on 6 months of historical eval data to estimate FP/FN rates and adjust thresholds accordingly.
Human review sample: create a 200-example stratified human-label set for tie-breaker decisions and store labeling guide in Drive.

source.title: Update PRD to convert reliability postmortem actions into runtime fallback requirements
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Background: During the Feb 24th production incident (INC-2026-02-24-kv-eviction), a cascade of events led to a sustained increase in request errors and tail latency for certain conversational workloads. Root causes identified in the postmortem: (1) an aggressive KV cache eviction policy that caused frequent cache misses for long-session customers, (2) routing logic failed to select a compatible fallback model variant when the primary GPU pool had transient memory exhaustion, and (3) missing high-priority alerts for fallback activation and error budget burn.

Goal: Translate the postmortem action items into explicit PRD updates so that engineering has clear acceptance criteria to implement: robust runtime fallback behavior, deterministic compatibility rules for model variants, improved signals/alerts, and rollout/rollback guards tied to SLOs and customer-impact thresholds.

Scope: This product ticket updates the PRD sections for runtime fallbacks, monitoring, and rollout policy. It defines requirements, non-goals, success metrics, and acceptance tests. Implementation work will be tracked in ENG tickets.

Non-goals: Changes to model training or model architecture, billing model changes, and re-architecting the serving runtime beyond well-scoped fallback control-plane changes.
requirements: Define runtime fallback policy language: priority list (primary -> fallback group -> ultimate degraded variant), compatibility constraints (architecture, tokenizer, max_seq_len), and per-customer override TTL.
Require atomic fallback activation with tracing: when a fallback path is taken, the request traces must include reason code (e.g., 'gpu_mem_pressure', 'kv_cache_miss_spike') and selected variant id.
Add a model-compatibility matrix to the PRD: for each supported model family list compatible fallback variants and expected behavior (latency, quality delta guidance).

Add SLO-driven rollback/abort rules: if fallback activation correlates with >2% increase in 95th-latency or >1% increase in error-rate over a 10-minute window for traffic routed to a rollout, auto-abort the rollout and trigger paging to on-call.
Define observability signals and alerts: new metrics fallback_rate, fallback_reason_breakdown, model_variant_mismatch_count, and per-route KV_miss_rate. Configure alert thresholds and runbooks.
Provide manual and automated canary flows: enable canary fraction config, minimum traffic to sample, and deterministic sampling for reproducible test runs.
Document customer impact annotation: when fallbacks occur mark tokens/requests so customers can filter logs and billing reports for impacted requests.
Require testing harness additions: unit + integration tests that simulate memory pressure, KV eviction spikes, and ensure fallback routes execute and are observable in trace logs.
Specify UX changes in Console: a per-route 'fallback' panel showing current policy, recent activations, and a one-click rollback for recent rollouts.
List security & compliance constraints: fallback logs must respect PII redaction policies and private deployment customers can opt-out of automated fallback to cross-region variants.
Acceptance criteria enumerated in the PRD must be automatable and mappable to ENG tickets for implementation verification.
acceptance_criteria: A1: Fallback policy language exists in the PRD with examples and a compatibility matrix; engineering can create a schema from it without follow-up clarifications.
A2: End-to-end demo documented: under simulated GPU memory pressure the system routes traffic to compatible fallback variant, traces contain reason code, and console shows activation within 30s.
A3: Three new metrics (fallback_rate, fallback_reason_breakdown, kv_miss_rate) are defined in the PRD with retention and cardinality guidance and example alert thresholds.
A4: Rollout abort criteria are specified and include clear numeric thresholds; a tabletop runbook exists that demonstrates abort behavior for a canary rollout.
A5: Console UX wireframes included in the PRD for fallback panel and one-click rollback; PM and Design have reviewed and signed off.
A6: Test list included: unit tests for policy parsing, integration test simulating eviction, and an e2e verification that logs tag impacted requests for customers.
A7: Security review notes included and opt-out behavior for Private deployments documented.
A8: Dependencies and ENG ticket mappings are enumerated so each PRD item links to implementation tickets before the PRD is approved.
notes: Postmortem reference: INC-2026-02-24-kv-eviction-postmortem (link in doc owner notes).
Related PRDs: runtime-fallbacks-v1 (archived) and PM-8421 (lessons-to-prd) – this ticket consolidates and operationalizes outstanding action items.
Designers: need wireframes for Console fallback panel — assigned to Priya Kaur.
Ops: define runbook for paging on rollback — consult with Oncall rotation owners (ENG-SRE team).
comments: 2026-03-10 Ava Chen: Drafted PRD updates from postmortem action list; focusing on deterministic fallback semantics and observability. Requesting initial review from Marco (runtime PM) and SRE leads.
2026-03-11 Marco Rivera: Added rollout abort numeric thresholds proposal and requested Design mockups for console panel. Blocking items: need compatibility matrix from Model Onboarding team (ENG-ML).
2026-03-12 Priya Kaur (Design): Will deliver 2 wireframe variants by 2026-03-17. Favor minimal telemetry surface to avoid overloading console.
2026-03-12 Jonah Park (SRE): Suggested adding synthetic canary job that periodically simulates KV-hit/miss patterns; this will be an ENG ticket. Marking as dependency.
open_questions: Q1: For Private deployments, should automatic cross-region fallback be disabled by default? (Security and data residency concerns).
Q2: What is the acceptable quality delta for fallback variants per model family (guideline for PM/ML leads to supply)?
Q3: Who owns the KMS/PII redaction checks in fallback traces for Private customers?
Q4: Should billing mark requests that experienced fallbacks differently for customer support reporting?

source.title: Tenant audit snapshot generator with PDF signing and CSV normalization
source.creator: Marina Alvarez
source.description:
Problem: Customers need audit-ready exports that combine residency-aware data selection, retention-safe redaction, and deterministic file formats (PDF with verifiable signing and canonical CSV rows) delivered on configurable schedules. Current export options are ad-hoc, produce inconsistent CSV column orders, and do not include a signed manifest that proves file provenance for audits.

Goal: Build a tenant-scoped snapshot generator that produces a packaged export per tenant containing: (1) a signed PDF report with table-of-contents and tenant-safe redactions; (2) canonicalized CSV datasets with stable column ordering and typed schema headers; (3) a machine-verifiable manifest and hash list (SHA-256) plus optional PKI-style signature for enterprise customers. Exports must respect regional residency filters and retention rules, and be deliverable via pre-signed S3 links and email webhook subscriptions.

Scope: MVP focuses on exports for: access logs, prompt/response evidence for a tenant, and billing/usage summaries. Support for other artifacts will be added via a plugin-style exporter. This ticket covers product definition, acceptance criteria, scheduling semantics, retention interaction, and delivery UX flows. Engineering work will be split into rendering service, signer/manifest, scheduler, and dashboard wiring.

Out-of-scope: full multi-tenant bundling across regions (will be follow-up), PII differential redaction policies that require legal review (out of MVP).
acceptance_criteria: Admins can create a recurring export policy for a tenant specifying: datasets, format (PDF/CSV), delivery channel (S3/email/webhook), time zone, and residency scope
Generated PDFs include a table of contents, standard header/footer with tenant id, and a machine-readable manifest embedded in PDF metadata
CSV files are canonicalized: stable column ordering, explicit schema header row, and consistent date/time formatting (ISO 8601 UTC)
Each export package contains a SHA-256 manifest and an optional PKI signature; for signed exports, signature verification script is published in repo
Exports enforce retention/redaction policies: records outside retention window are omitted or redacted per tenant policy
Dashboard shows last 6 exports, status (success/failure), size, residency region, and provides immediate re-run/download
Scheduler supports retry/backoff and admin notification on repeated failures
tasks: Finalize PRD and security checklist (PM + Legal)
Design PDF template and CSV canonical schema (Design + PM)
Implement export renderer service (Eng) - PDF and CSV producer
Add manifest generation and signing module (Eng + Security)
Scheduling engine extension to support tenant cron and timezone semantics (Eng)
Console UI: export policy creation flow and exports table (Product + Design)
Integrate residency filters and retention enforcement in export pipeline (Eng)
Add CI validation for CSV schema and PDF metadata (CI)
End-to-end QA: regression tests, performance under large tenants (QA)
comments: 2026-02-15 - Marina Alvarez: Kickoff meeting notes attached to confluence. Agreed to prioritize signed manifests for enterprise tenants.
2026-02-20 - Ethan Park: Scoped CSV canonicalization rules. We will adopt typed headers and ISO timestamps. Design to provide mockups for PDF TOC by 2026-02-25.
2026-02-27 - Priya Nanda (Security): PKI signing approach approved for Private customers; recommend using HSM-backed keys for key material. Need security review in next sprint.
2026-03-03 - Design review: PDF template v1 completed; table-of-contents will group sections by dataset with anchor links. Small change: increase font size for audit readability.
2026-03-10 - Engineering update (Ethan): Export renderer prototype produces canonical CSV and unsigned manifest. Working on integrating signer. Scheduled demo for 2026-03-18.
2026-03-11 - PM note: Confirm retention interactions with retention engine (PM-24198). If retention removes records, include a redaction summary in the manifest.
notes: Trade-offs: Embedded PDF signing increases CPU and latency at export time; we will support asynchronous signing with post-processing for non-enterprise customers to reduce peak load.
Localization: Dates will be stored in UTC in CSV to avoid formatting ambiguities; PDFs will include local timezone label where requested.
Privacy: For tenants that opt into 'minimal-evidence' exports, replace PII fields with stable pseudonyms and include a pseudonym mapping ledger protected by restricted access.
Operational concerns: Large tenants may produce multi-GB packages; ensure scheduler has quota and chunked upload support. Consider incremental exports (delta) as follow-up.

source.title: Milestone cadence, scope-lockdown windows, and emergency rollback runway for API / Console / Runtime
source.creator: Lena Morales
source.description: Goal: produce a single, actionable milestone cadence and a clear scope-lockdown + rollback runway tailored to the three-component release (API, Console, Runtime). Deliverables: (1) a published milestone calendar with freeze windows and owners, (2) a living cut-criteria matrix that maps severity -> allowed scope, (3) an emergency rollback runbook and Canary windows schedule, and (4) a release-notes coordination plan and templates for PM/Eng/Design/GTM.
