source.title: Segmented Slab Reserves and Proportional Burst Allocation (PRD)
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Overview:
This PRD proposes a new allocation model for Dedicated capacity called 'Segmented Slab Reserves' (SSRs) paired with a Proportional Burst Allocation (PBA) mechanism. SSRs divide a tenant's reserved committed capacity into multiple fixed-size slabs that can be independently used, released, or prewarmed. PBA provides a bounded, auditable burst runway that draws proportional credits from a global burst pool and decays over time.

Motivation and problem statement:
Current reserved-slot models are coarse-grained (single contiguous reservation per tenant) which causes: wasted headroom on uneven load patterns, poor isolation when tenants spike, and brittle autoscaling when combined with short-lived bursts. Dedicated customers want: predictable baseline throughput, controlled temporary burst above baseline, and transparent accounting for burst usage. Operators need easier reclamation and prewarming strategies that don't destabilize other tenants.

This PRD focuses on engineering and product requirements for SSR + PBA to improve utilization, reduce surprise preemption, and offer explicit burst contracts with clear billing signals.
source.goals:
- Reduce wasted reserved capacity by enabling tenants to split committed capacity into independently-managed slabs.
- Offer a bounded burst runway with proportional crediting that is transparent in billing and telemetry.
- Provide graceful preemption and isolation controls: soft-fence, drain-with-grace and hard-evict policies.
- Maintain latency SLOs for baseline traffic while allowing controlled bursts.
- Deliver operator tooling to visualize slab occupancy, burst credit, and reclaim candidates.
source.non_goals:
- This work does not redesign global scheduling or replace existing dedicated reservation contracts.
- Not addressing multi-region placement strategies (out-of-scope).
- Not implementing new low-level GPU kernel changes — runtime integration points only.
- Not changing hosted API pricing models beyond adding explicit burst metrics for billing clarity.
source.success_metrics:
- Increase average reserved capacity utilization by ≥12% for participating tenants within 60 days of rollout.
- <1% of baseline requests experience latency > SLA due to SSR/PBA launch.
- Burst credit overrun incidents (where tenant exceeds cap) reduced to 0.
- Customer-reported surprises related to preemption drop by 90% in NPS/CSAT follow-ups for pilot cohort.
- Telemetry surfaces slab-level metrics in Console with <2s freshness for top 10 tenants.
source.requirements:
Functional requirements:
1) Slab definition: tenants can split a single reservation into N slabs (N up to 16), each slab defined by: slab_id, vGPU_count, prewarm_policy (always|on-demand|scheduled), fence_level (soft|medium|hard).
2) Proportional Burst Pool: a global pool funded by operator-configurable percentage of idle capacity plus a tenant-specific committed burst multiplier. Burst draw uses proportional weighting by tenant QoS weight.
3) Credit accounting: per-tenant burst ledger that decrements on burst consumption and replenishes at configured rates (credits/min).
4) Autoscale handshake: when slab usage exceeds threshold T_high, system triggers a prewarm request for adjacent slabs or evicts opportunistic backfill; if sustained >T_critical, escalate preemption per fence policy.
5) Fallback routing: requests should be rerouted to a queued fallback (shared-hosted tier or dedicated lower-QoS slab) with transparent error codes and telemetry.
6) Operator controls: UI to split/merge slabs, schedule prewarm windows, set tenant QoS weight, and reclaim slabs with audit logging.
7) Billing & reporting: burst usage appears as a separate line item with timestamps and consumed credits; Console shows predicted future credit burn at historic QPS.
8) API/SDK: new endpoints to query slab status, burst credits, and request ad-hoc temporary slab increases (subject to policy).

Non-functional requirements:
- Control plane changes must not add >200ms median request path latency.
- Telemetry ingestion of slab events should scale to 50k ops/sec with 99th percentile latency <5s.
- All changes must preserve existing SLAs for Dedicated baseline capacity.
- Access controls: only tenant admins + designated operator roles can modify slab allocations.
acceptance_criteria: Create Slab: Product API accepts create_slab request and returns slab_id; slab appears in Console under tenant reserved capacity; slab attributes match request.
Split/Merge: Tenant can split a reservation into 2+ slabs and later merge back with no loss of baseline throughput when merge is completed.
Burst Draw: When tenant burst occurs, credits are debited in proportional manner and logged in billing pipeline; burst throttle enforces configured burst cap hard limit.
Prewarm Workflow: Prewarm requests for adjacent slabs are initiated within T_prewarm_target (<=30s) when threshold breached and either succeed or fail with a deterministic fallback action recorded.
Isolation Behavior: Soft-fence drains connections over configured grace period; hard-evict immediately reclaims resources and emits incident event; both behaviors tested via chaos scenario.
Telemetry: Console displays slab occupancy, burst credits, and predicted burn rate; alert fires when tenant burn rate projected to exhaust credits in <10 minutes.
Billing: Burst usage appears in billing CSV and matches ledger within 1% discrepancy in end-to-end tests over 24h simulated load.
Security: Only authorized roles can change slab configuration; audit log contains user, timestamp, and before/after state for slab changes.
Performance: No regression >1% on baseline P50/P95 latency for dedicated workloads in canary region under representative load.
Documentation: PRD, operator runbook, Console copy, API spec, and sample SDK snippet published and reviewed by PM, Eng, and Design.
Pilot: Feature is enabled for at least 3 pilot tenants, with telemetry and customer feedback collected for two billing cycles before wider rollout.
source.api_changes:
New API endpoints (REST + SDK):
- POST /v1/dedicated/tenants/{tenant_id}/slabs  => create slab with JSON body {vGPU_count, prewarm_policy, fence_level, name}.
- GET /v1/dedicated/tenants/{tenant_id}/slabs => list slabs, occupancy, credits.
- POST /v1/dedicated/tenants/{tenant_id}/burst/request => request ad-hoc burst; response includes estimated cost and acceptance boolean.
- GET /v1/dedicated/tenants/{tenant_id}/burst/ledger => returns burst credit history (paginated).
- POST /v1/operator/reclaim_slab => operator-initiated reclaim with justification and dry-run mode.
API semantics: idempotent create calls supported; admin-only operations require elevated token scope.
source.console_and_ui_changes:
Console additions:
- Tenant -> Capacity tab shows slab table with per-slab occupancy, prewarm status, fence level, and 'request burst' CTA.
- Topline widgets: total reserved slabs, current burst burn rate, projected exhaustion ETA.
- Operator view: reclaim candidates list with heuristic scores (idle_time, last_used, prewarm_cost).
- Alert surfaces in Console and email when projected exhaustion <10 minutes.
Design notes: design will provide compact slab row UI and a timeline view for slab prewarm/eviction history.
source.infra_requirements:
- Scheduler integration: ability to mark slabs as prewarmed and to preempt opportunistic backfill tasks.
- Global burst pool service: maintain credits, proportional weighting, and persistent ledger (RDS-backed).
- Telemetry pipeline: new slab/burst event types; retention for 90 days at high granularity for pilot tenants.
- Storage: slab metadata stored in config DB with versioning for audit.
- Feature flag gating for pilot by tenant_id and regional toggles.
- Backfill policies for opportunistic workloads need minor extension: tags to indicate revocable occupancy.
source.rollout_plan:
Phase 1 (Pilot - 4 weeks): enable SSR+PBA for 3 conservative tenants in staging and canary region; collect metrics, iterate.
Phase 2 (Limited GA - 6 weeks): open to self-service for tenants under 1k vGPU-hours/mo with operator opt-in; enable billing lines and console visibility.
Phase 3 (GA - 4 weeks): full rollout with migration tooling for large tenants; provide migration consultations and opt-in window.
Feature flag will default to disabled; automated migration scripts required for tenants wanting to convert monolithic reservations to slabs.
source.telemetry_and_alerts:
Events to emit: slab_created, slab_split, slab_merged, slab_prewarm_start/stop, slab_evicted, burst_draw, burst_alert_projected_exhaustion.
Key dashboards: slab occupancy heatmap, burst credit burn leaderboard, pilot tenant SLA dashboard.
Alerts: projected_exhaustion(<10m), prewarm_fail_rate(>5% in 10m), slab_eviction_rate(>0.5% per tenant/hour).
Retention: 90d detailed, 13 months aggregated for billing reconciliation.
source.risks_and_mitigations:
Risk: Misconfigured slab sizes could fragment tenant throughput and increase latency. Mitigation: provide recommended slab sizing heuristics and 'auto-suggest' in Console based on historical token distribution.
Risk: Burst pool starvation or noisy neighbor during massive correlated spikes. Mitigation: enforce tenant-level hard cap and proportional weighting; emergency global throttles and operator override.
Risk: Billing confusion from separate burst line items. Mitigation: clear Console labels, sample invoices, and customer-facing FAQ + onboarding calls during pilot.
Risk: Operator tooling complexity. Mitigation: phased UI with limited operator actions initially, expand after pilot feedback.
source.open_questions:
- What is the minimum slab granularity we should support for enterprise tenants with large reservations? (8 vGPU vs 16 vGPU?)
- Should credits be transferable between slabs under same tenant or strictly pooled?
- How to price burst credits for long-tail usage vs short spikes? Flat rate per credit or time-weighted pricing?
- Should we expose 'auto-consolidate' that merges nearly-empty slabs automatically?
- How to present preemption impact to callers (error codes, retry-after headers, structured telemetry)?

title: Segmented Slab Reserves and Proportional Burst Allocation (PRD)
preview:
Overview:
This PRD proposes a new allocation model for Dedicated capacity called 'Segmented Slab Reserves' (SSRs) paired with a Proportional Burst Allocation (PBA) mechanism. SSRs divide a tenant's reserved committed capacity into multiple fixed-size slabs that can be independently used, released, or prewarmed. PBA provides a bounded, auditable burst runway that draws proportional credits from a global burst pool and decays over time.

Motivation and problem statement:
Current reserved-slot models are coarse-grained (single contiguous reservation per tenant) which causes: wasted headroom on uneven load patterns, poor isolation when tenants spike, and brittle autoscaling when combined with short-lived bursts. Dedicated customers want: predictable baseline throughput, controlled temporary burst above baseline, and transparent accounting for burst usage. Operators need easier reclamation and prewarming strategies that don't destabilize other tenants.

This PRD focuses on engineering and product requirements for SSR + PBA to improve utilization, reduce surprise preemption, and offer explicit burst contracts with clear billing signals.

source.title: Probabilistic fallback prioritization & experiment roadmap for model availability
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Objective: build a defensible, data-driven playbook and A/B experiment roadmap that ranks candidate fallback models probabilistically (probability of acceptable output) given observed signals from the model landscape and customer workload patterns. The playbook's goal is to minimize customer-visible regressions while optimizing token cost and latency across Hosted/Dedicated/Private deployments.

Context: customers expect graceful degradation when primary models are throttled, change, or become unavailable. Current fallback heuristics are ad hoc (static prioritization by latency then cost). Market research and competitive scans show rivals exposing model variant guarantees and automated fallback tiers; we need a formalized approach that ties competitive signals, regional availability, quantization compatibility, and empirical quality regressions into a single prioritization engine.
source.acceptance_criteria: 1) A documented prioritization algorithm with inputs and output ranking semantics published to Confluence. 2) A simulation harness that consumes historical traffic and model availability traces and produces expected customer-impact metrics (false-positive regressions, average cost delta, latency delta). 3) A canary experiment plan for one enterprise customer with rollback criteria and SLO thresholds. 4) A decision log capturing tradeoffs (cost vs quality vs latency) and final rollout policy.
source.research_plan: - Collect 90 days of traffic traces and model variant availability events (ENG infra dataset). - Run competitive feature synthesis to map how top-10 suppliers surface fallback guarantees (PM research doc). - Interview 6 platform customers (success + eng contacts) to capture tolerance for hallucination/regression and acceptable latency/cost thresholds. - Build synthetic workloads for 3 archetypes: low-latency chat, high-throughput summarization, and embedding-based search; measure candidate fallback behavior under constrained capacity. - Run Monte Carlo simulations to estimate probability distributions of quality loss and cost delta per fallback rank.
source.decision_log:
- 2026-03-05: Decided to use a probabilistic scoring approach (Bayesian ranking) instead of deterministic rule-based cascades; rationale: captures uncertainty and adapts as we observe outcomes. Owner: Marco Ruiz.
- 2026-03-09: Chose seed candidate set: {redwood-v1-small-quant, community-gpt-3.7-q4, redwood-v1-medium} for initial experiments to cover cost/latency/quality tradeoffs. Owner: Research.
- 2026-03-11: Opted to surface an explainability score (why fallback selected) in the Console rollout UI to aid SRE and customers. Owner: Console PM.
source.risks_and_mitigations: - Risk: Simulation fidelity — synthetic workloads may under/overestimate real regressions. Mitigation: run a two-week shadow canary on live traffic for opt-in customers. - Risk: Private deployments have restricted model variants composition. Mitigation: add an infra capability check step and fallback to regional-compatible variants. - Risk: Quantized weights can cause silent regressions. Mitigation: include quant-compat checks and a 'quality hold' flag that prevents fallback until sanity evals pass.
source.experiment_plan:
Phase 0: Lab sims (2 weeks) — run Monte Carlo with historical traces; dashboard: fallback-sim-dashboard.
Phase 1: Shadow canary (4 weeks) — route 5% of traffic through fallback engine, no customer-facing switch; collect quality/latency/cost.
Phase 2: Controlled rollout (2 weeks) — A/B test to 10% customers with immediate rollback if >2% regression in end-to-end metrics.
Phase 3: Enterprise pilot (4 weeks) — enable for 1 enterprise account with 100% switch over during short maintenance windows.
Metrics: delta_accept_rate, query_level_latency_p95, token_cost_delta, customer_complaints, rollback_incidents.
comments: 2026-03-03 - Ava Chen: Ticket created after field signal from CS about two enterprise customers seeing poor fallback choices during a model outage.
2026-03-05 - Marco Ruiz: Initial tech spike completed — prototype ranking function implemented. Results: tradeoff curve shows 12% lower cost at 1.8% quality delta for synthetic chat workload.
2026-03-06 - Priya Nair: Research synthesis added competitive signal layer; several providers expose regional fallback tiers which we should incorporate into the candidate scoring.
2026-03-08 - Lucas Kwan: Console team needs a UX wireframe for explainability callouts by 2026-03-15 to align with Phase 1 instrumentation.
2026-03-09 - Elena Morales: Scheduling customer interviews; 4 completed, 2 pending. Early feedback: enterprise customers prefer conservative fallbacks prioritized by quality over cost.
2026-03-10 - Marco Ruiz: Blocked on historical traces access (ENG-Logs role). Submitted infra request ENG-1842.
2026-03-11 - Ava Chen: Prioritized canary criteria updated to include automated rollback on >3 regression tickets within a 48h window.
2026-03-12 - Marco Ruiz: Lab sims completed; uploading artifacts to Drive and raising PR ops/pull/4136 for review.
source.next_steps: 1) Resolve infra access (follow up on ENG-1842) and hand over sanitized traces to Research. 2) Deliver Console explainability wireframes (Lucas) by 2026-03-15. 3) Run Phase 0 sims and publish results to Confluence by 2026-03-19. 4) Prepare shadow canary runbook and legal/enterprise opt-in checklist by 2026-03-24.

source.title: Access request orchestration & triage protocol for enterprise admin features
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Create a repeatable orchestration and prioritization protocol for non-bug admin & access management requests (RBAC policies, SSO/SAML onboarding, SCIM provisioning, org-level policy adjustments). Goal: reduce manual PM engineering routing, ensure high-risk enterprise requests are fast-tracked, and produce a signal-to-priority mapping that can be automated in the intake layer.
source.context_and_problem: We currently receive enterprise admin requests via Support, Sales, and CSMs. Many are routed ad-hoc to engineers or ops leads which causes inconsistent SLAs and duplicated work. Recent enterprise wins have increased volume of SSO/SCIM/RBAC changes; without a clear triage protocol we risk escalations, security gaps, and slow onboarding times. This ticket captures a product-managed protocol (signals, thresholds, routing) and a companion prioritization matrix for intake engineers and partner teams.
acceptance_criteria: Documented triage rubric mapping signal categories to priority buckets (P0/P1/P2) and SLA targets
Routing rules defined for automated labeling and assignment to teams (Support->CSM->PM->Eng->Security) with handoff SLAs
A sample webhook/payload shape and Confluence spec for intake tooling with required fields for automation
Pilot triage run with 8 incoming enterprise requests demonstrating correct labeling and route decisions
Stakeholder sign-off from Security, Sales Engineering, and Head of Customer Success
triage_rubric: Customer tier weight: Enterprise (2x), Midmarket (1.2x), Self-serve (0.7x)
Urgency modifiers: deployment_blocker => force P0, security_incident => immediate P0 and pager to Security
Final score thresholds: >=7 => P0 (SLA 24h), 4-6 => P1 (SLA 3 business days), 1-3 => P2 (SLA 10 business days)
routing_flow: 1) Intake form (Support/CSM/Sales) populates required fields: customer_id, tier, request_type, sample_config, compliance_notes
2) Automation service applies triage_rubric and sets priority, labels (sso/scim/rbac/security), and assigns to queue: support-triage or enterprise-ops
3) Queued tickets: if score >=7 => create immediate cross-team sync (PM + Eng owner + Security) and schedule 1-hour kickoff
4) For P1: assign to product manager (assignee) and engineer on-call for design review. For P2: route to product backlog with automated confirmation to requestor
5) Escalation path: if not picked up within SLA threshold, auto-escalate to Head of Customer Success and Eng Manager
sample_payload_requirements: attached_artifacts (links to IdP metadata, SCIM mapping, audit logs)
decisions_and_tradeoffs: Decision: use score-based triage (numeric) vs fixed taxonomy routing. Tradeoff: numeric scoring is flexible but requires tuning; taxonomy is simpler but brittle. Chosen: numeric with prescriptive thresholds.
Decision: automate initial routing to reduce human latency. Tradeoff: risk of mislabeling; mitigations include mandatory required fields and fallback manual-review queue.
Decision: fast-track enterprise tier with multiplier. Tradeoff: may starve team capacity for other customers; mitigation: capacity guardrails and monthly allocation review.
open_questions: What minimum set of IdP metadata do we require to reliably classify SAML/SSO complexity?
Should integrations with Support/Intercom create tickets that mirror Linear items or link back to a centralized intake service?
How do we instrument triage accuracy and tune weights — monthly review vs automated feedback loop?
pilot_plan: Week 0: finalize rubric and payload spec; build intake template for Support and Sales Engineering
Week 1: run pilot on 8 live enterprise requests and log decisions and manual overrides
Week 2: review pilot outcomes with Security and Customer Success; tune thresholds and labels
Week 3: implement automated routing in intake service and add observability dashboards (accuracy, SLA compliance)
metrics_and_success: Mean time to first response for P0/P1 requests (target: P0 < 4h, P1 < 24h)
Percentage of correctly auto-labeled requests (target > 90% after tuning)
Reduction in manual re-routes by Support (target 60% reduction)
Time-to-onboard enterprise SSO customers (target reduce from 9 days to 4 days)
notes_and_progress_updates: 2026-03-01 - Ava Chen: Initial draft of protocol and rubric. Captured common customer asks from Sales and Support for review.
2026-03-04 - Priya Kapoor: Added urgency modifiers; flagged need for Security sign-off on risk scoring.
2026-03-08 - Marcus Holloway (Security): Requested explicit handling for 'audit_required' and EU data residency signals.
2026-03-10 - Tom Nguyen (Support): Confirmed Support can add required fields to intake form; action: Support to add three required fields by 2026-03-15.
2026-03-12 - Priya Kapoor: Updated SLA targets and published pilot plan. Ready for pilot coordination with Sales Eng.
next_steps: Finalize the intake template and send to Support and Sales Engineering for implementation (owner: Priya)
Security to review and sign off on risk scores and EU/UK modifiers (owner: Marcus)
Engineering to scope the lightweight automation for scoring and routing (owner: Ravi, dependency ENG-20344)
Schedule pilot intake window and define success criteria (owner: Priya, target start 2026-03-16)

source.title: Staffing Mosaic & Graceful Trim Playbook for Cycle Allocation
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Objective: Create a pragmatic, ops-friendly playbook that helps PMs and Eng managers size a cycle by composing a "mosaic" of staffing slices (core commits, surge lanes, on-call buffer, and cross-functional steady-state) and a lightweight graceful-trim decision flow for mid-cycle scope cuts. The playbook should be actionable in a 30–60 minute triage workshop, integrate with existing OKR priorities, and produce a prioritized scope list plus a staffing allocation sheet that can be consumed by the Capacity Board and CTO review. Deliverables: 1) One-page staffing mosaic template (CSV + Google Sheet), 2) Graceful Trim decision flow (checklist + scoring matrix), 3) Example run through for two sample initiatives (runtime perf and console UX), 4) Templates for Slack/Notion update and approval workflow. Out of scope: replacing formal resource-allocation policy; this is a tactical triage/playbook for product cycles.
acceptance_criteria: Runbook exists in Notion with step-by-step triage agenda and artifacts
Staffing mosaic template produces a clear allocation (% FTE or points) for core/surge/ops buffers for two sample initiatives
Graceful trim flow yields a prioritized cut list that reduces scope by targeted percent while preserving at least 70% of OKR objective impact
Stakeholder sign-off from Eng leads (Liam O'Connor), Design (Sofia Ruiz), and GTM (Diego Morales)
Board-ready one-pager and a sample spreadsheet exported for Capacity Board review
steps: Feb 25: Kickoff with Eng/Product/Design to align on goals and constraints
Feb 27 - Mar 3: Pull historical cycle velocity, commit deltas, and cost-of-delay signals from analytics
Mar 4: Draft staffing mosaic template and initial scoring matrix for trim decisions
Mar 6: Cross-functional tradeoff workshop (60m) — capture edge-cases and guardrails
Mar 9: Iterate runbook with feedback and create two worked examples
Mar 11: Present to Capacity Board for feedback
Mar 12: Incorporate board feedback and prepare final artifacts for pilot in Cycle 2026-W11
updates: 2026-02-25 - Ava Chen: Created ticket after kickoff; captured scope and initial success metrics.
2026-03-01 - Priya Patel: Pulled last 6 cycles velocity and average unplanned work delta; noted 12% average mid-cycle scope creep.
2026-03-04 - Liam O'Connor (Eng): Reviewed draft template; suggested adding an 'ops-reserve' line for infra/incident response that is non-negotiable.

2026-03-06 - Cross-functional workshop notes: Agreement to use a 3-band trim severity (High/Medium/Low) and to prefer cutting 'low-customer-impact' items first. Action: Priya to populate worked examples.
2026-03-09 - Diego Morales (GTM): Requested explicit mapping from trimmed items to OKR progression to avoid inadvertent sales blockers.

2026-03-11 - Capacity Board review: Positive; requested two changes — (1) clearer escalation path when trims hit >25% scope reduction, (2) automated extraction of affected OKR progress for transparency. Priya to action both.
2026-03-12 - Priya Patel: Updated runbook with escalation path and added a lightweight script to annotate affected OKR checkboxes in Notion. Preparing pilot communications.
Next steps: Pilot the playbook in Cycle 2026-W11 with runtime perf squad and console UX squad; collect feedback and measure time-to-decision and % scope trimmed vs. expected.
risks: Teams may treat playbook as prescriptive and not adapt to local context (mitigation: emphasize checklist nature and require one-off deviations to be documented).
Automated OKR annotation script could mis-tag items if naming conventions vary (mitigation: include a manual verification step and a pattern checklist).
If trims exceed 25% there is a risk to roadmap credibility — escalate to CTO and Head of Product per playbook.
decisions: Use a mosaic that splits allocation into: Core (60%), Surge (20%), Ops reserve (10%), Contingency Pool (10%) as a starting default; teams can adjust with justification.
Trim scoring will weight: customer-impact (40%), parity with OKR (30%), engineering effort/risks (20%), external blockers (10%).
Pilot to run with two squads in 2026-W11 before a wider roll out; success gate is <48h average decision time and >50% adoption of runbook checklist by participating teams.

source.title: Reserve Safelist and Burst Scheduler for Dedicated Capacity
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Problem: Customers on Dedicated capacity sometimes experience noisy-neighbor interference during transient bursts. Current reserved pool semantics provide hard reservations and vague soft-burst behavior, but we lack a lightweight mechanism to guarantee minimal forward progress for a small group of mission-critical tenants while still allowing elastic burst traffic.

This PRD proposes two coordinated features: (1) a Reserve Safelist: a configurable micro-reservation that guarantees a tiny, constantly-available slice of capacity for nominated tenants (useful for latency-sensitive endpoints and compliance workloads); and (2) a Burst Scheduler: a credit-based short-window scheduler that fairly mediates transient burst traffic across tenants, integrates with existing reserved pools and autoscaling triggers, and supports graceful preemption and cooldown windows. The combined goal is to improve p95/p99 latency for critical tenants during noisy windows while preserving overall cluster efficiency.
source.scope:
IN SCOPE:
- Design and implement Reserve Safelist configuration (tenant-level flag + allocation size) in the Dedicated control plane.
- Implement Burst Scheduler in the serving layer that: credit-scores incoming requests, enforces per-tenant short-term rate windows, and exposes telemetry for troubleshooting.
- Preemption rules: safelist slices are protected from eviction by standard soft-eviction for X minutes.
- Console UI and API surfaces for PMs/owners to manage safelist entries and view burst credit state.
- Load testing and perf benchmarks against baseline.

OUT OF SCOPE:
- Changes to long-term reserved pool billing model.
- Global autoscaler redesign; this will integrate with existing autoscale hooks only.
- Deep quota billing changes beyond metering telemetry.
acceptance_criteria: Safelist can be configured per-tenant via API and Console with min and max slice values (e.g., 0.5-2 GPU-shares equivalent).
During a synthetic noisy-neighbor test (90th-99th percentile synthetic burst), safelisted tenants retain >= 95% of baseline QPS and p99 latency degradation < 2x baseline for 10-minute windows.
Burst Scheduler enforces credit ceilings and cooldowns: tenants cannot exceed allowed burst credits more than 3x sustained baseline for >30s without triggering backpressure or preemption events.
Metrics exposed: safelist_allocated, safelist_evictions, burst_credit_balance, burst_preemptions, burst_throttles; visible in Console per-tenant timeline.
Integration tests that simulate autoscale handoff (burst -> autoscale -> scale-down) without violating safelist guarantees.
Documentation and runbook updated; oncall runbook includes steps to revert safelist entries and inspect burst credit history.
Security review completed and audit logs available for safelist changes (who created/removed entries, timestamps).
source.metrics_and_kpis: Primary: p99 latency for safelisted tenants during noisy window (target < 2x baseline). Secondary: safelist availability (goal 99.9% monthly), percent of bursts resolved by scheduler without requiring manual intervention (>80%), autoscale handoffs without customer-impact incidents. Cost KPI: incremental GPU-hours used by safelist slices should be <= 3% of total cluster capacity in typical traffic patterns.
decision_log: 2026-02-22: Chosen credit-based burst scheduler (over fixed token-bucket on ingress) because it better integrates with backend KV cache and allows credit carry-forward during short gaps — decision by PM/ENG/Research.

2026-02-28: Safelist size will be expressed in 'effective GPU-shares' for portability between hosted and dedicated; mapped to instance-level preemption tokens at runtime. Agreed to surface both share and approximate qps estimate in Console. (Design + Eng)

2026-03-05: Preemption policy: safelist slices are immune to soft-evictions for initial 5 minutes after allocation, then subject to controlled yield with 30s drain window. Rationale: reduce churn while avoiding permanent starvation. (SRE + Eng)

2026-03-10: Opted to postpone billing model changes; safelist will be billed as a conservative multiplier on existing reserved fees and tracked via telemetry for a future pricing proposal. (Finance + PM)
source.tradeoffs:
- Guarantee vs. Efficiency: Safelists improve isolation but reserve capacity that may sit idle; we minimize waste by allowing tiny slice sizes and by making safelist allocations elastic within a small band.
- Complexity vs. Observability: Credit system adds moving parts; we prioritize explicit telemetry and simple debug endpoints to avoid black-box behavior.
- Autoscale interaction: We avoid deep changes to autoscaler to reduce rollout risk; consequence is some transient periods where autoscaler lag may cause safelist guarantees to be suboptimal until warmup completes.
source.rollout_plan:
Phase 0 (canary): implement safelist flags and no-op scheduler in staging; enable for 2 internal test tenants.
Phase 1 (limited beta): enable safelist + burst scheduler for up to 10 paying customers with SLO contracts. Monitor for 2 weeks.
Phase 2: incremental ramp to all dedicated customers with feature flag per-account.
Phase 3: GA with Console UI and documented billing note.
Rollback: remove safelist entries and disable scheduler mode; autoscale falls back to baseline behavior.
Monitoring: add alerting on safelist_evictions > 1% and burst_preemptions spikes > baseline * 3.
source.testing_plan: Unit tests for credit accounting and preemption state. Integration tests simulating mixed workload (steady + bursty). Performance runs with 50+ tenant simulations to verify scheduler fairness. Chaos test: randomly kill worker nodes to validate preemption/cooperative-drain semantics. Post-deploy canary: traffic shift to canary cluster with safelist enabled for 72 hours before wider rollout.
open_questions: What default safelist slice size should be recommended for common enterprise workloads (100 qps vs 1000 qps)?
Should safelist be billable as a separate line item immediately or tracked as a usage multiplier for 2 quarters?
How does safelist interact with Dedicated fairshare when multiple safelisted tenants exist in the same zone?
Do we provide an API for auto-suggesting safelist sizes based on historical telemetry?

2026-03-11 (Security): Marta requests audit log retention of 90 days for safelist changes; security review flag toggled to 'true' and compliance tasks added.

title: Reserve Safelist and Burst Scheduler for Dedicated Capacity
preview:
Problem: Customers on Dedicated capacity sometimes experience noisy-neighbor interference during transient bursts. Current reserved pool semantics provide hard reservations and vague soft-burst behavior, but we lack a lightweight mechanism to guarantee minimal forward progress for a small group of mission-critical tenants while still allowing elastic burst traffic.

This PRD proposes two coordinated features: (1) a Reserve Safelist: a configurable micro-reservation that guarantees a tiny, constantly-available slice of capacity for nominated tenants (useful for latency-sensitive endpoints and compliance workloads); and (2) a Burst Scheduler: a credit-based short-window scheduler that fairly mediates transient burst traffic across tenants, integrates with existing reserved pools and autoscaling triggers, and supports graceful preemption and cooldown windows. The combined goal is to improve p95/p99 latency for critical tenants during noisy windows while preserving overall cluster efficiency.

source.title: Inbound open-model risk bucketing and staged rollout contract
source.creator: Maya Rodriguez
source.description: Clarify requirements for how new open (community) model submissions are classified by risk, what metadata gates they must include, and the rollout contract that binds pinning, telemetry gating, and staged percentage exposure. This PRD fragment is intended to: 1) define discrete risk buckets (Low/Moderate/High) with concrete signal thresholds; 2) specify minimum intake metadata required to accept an unpinned variant; 3) prescribe a staged rollout plan with telemetry gates and automatic rollback triggers; and 4) document operator responsibilities and handoffs to SRE/security for final admission. Goal is to give engineering clear pass/fail criteria for the intake pipeline and a deterministic rollout contract for Catalog and Runtime teams.
acceptance_criteria: Define 3 risk buckets (LOW/MODERATE/HIGH) with exact numeric thresholds for: latency percentile, token-level error rate proxy (top-k mismatch in eval harness), and hallucination proxy (entity hallucination rate on canonical test prompts).

For LOW risk submissions: allow unpinned entry if submitter provides required metadata (model hash, provenance URL, quantization profile, inference footprint estimates) and passes automated sanity: runtime load test <= 90% of regional SLOs and eval regression <= 5% relative to baseline.
For MODERATE risk submissions: require soft-pinning for first 30 days with 0.5% traffic cap and mandatory canary metrics for 72 hours before increasing exposure.
For HIGH risk submissions: require pinned deployment (customer-facing pin), security review sign-off, and explicit rollback plan; no unpinned entry allowed.

Staged rollout contract must enumerate telemetry gates (latency P95, 99; error rate; quality regression on golden prompts) and exact rollback thresholds (e.g., latency P95 > 1.5x baseline for 10m or quality regression > 10% on rolling 1h window).
Automated admission checks must abort intake and flag a ticket when required metadata fields are missing; UI should show reason codes and next steps.
SRE/Runtime must have an automated fallback route preconfigured to a verified pinned variant when rollout gates fail; the Catalog must record the fallback variant id and timestamp.
Doc links (Confluence intake checklist), a test eval harness (CI job), and a perf dashboard must exist and be linked in the intake UI before landing this contract.
decisions: Adopt three-tier risk bucket model instead of continuous score to keep human review tractable (Decision logged 2026-02-18).
Require provenance URL and model hash for any unpinned variant admitted to catalog (PM + Legal consensus 2026-02-22).
Staged rollout exposures: LOW = can be unpinned and eligible for immediate 5% baseline routing; MODERATE = soft-pin with 0.5% initial exposure; HIGH = must be pinned (2026-02-26).
Telemetry rollup window set to rolling 10-minute buckets for rollback calculations to avoid flapping (Engineering proposal accepted 2026-03-03).

[Eng] Implement intake metadata validation service and return actionable error codes (owner: ENG-21420) - Estimate 3 pts
[SRE] Implement automated fallback routing policy and smoke test in staging (owner: ENG-21789) - Estimate 2 pts
[Design] Update Catalog UI to surface risk bucket, required fields, and rollback history (owner: Priya Desai) - Add mockups to drive link
[PM] Coordinate security review and update acceptance criteria based on findings - block until review clears

2026-02-16 Ethan Park (ENG): Engineering feasibility review - intake metadata validation is straightforward; canary automation needs SRE involvement. Flagged a dependency on ENG-21789.
2026-02-18 Priya Desai (Design): Provided two UI patterns for showing risk buckets inline with model entry. Preferred a compact badge + details flyout to reduce clutter.
2026-02-22 Legal (Ana Gomez): Approved requiring provenance URL and model hash for unpinned variants; suggested retention of provenance links for 1 year for auditability.
2026-03-03 SRE (Noah Kim): Proposed rolling 10-minute window for gate evaluation to reduce false positives; agreed to implement automated fallback in staging first.

2026-03-09 Security (Leila Hassan): Initial review flagged potential exfil risk for unpinned community weights; recommended additional KMS integration note and 'do not auto-promote' flag for unknown provenance.
2026-03-12 Maya Rodriguez: Incorporated security feedback and updated acceptance criteria; moving ticket to In Review pending final eng estimate and mock sign-off.

source.title: Client onramp prioritization rubric — language coverage, auth patterns & streaming ergonomics
source.creator: Aisha Kumar
source.description:
Problem: We receive a steady stream of customer requests for new language client libraries and auth/streaming ergonomics changes. Without a consistent intake and prioritization rubric we over-index on vocal requests and create engineering churn. This ticket defines a repeatable rubric and intake flow for triaging SDK/client requests with a focus on enterprise needs (auth patterns, token rotation, SSO/OIDC, resumable streaming, backpressure) and language coverage trade-offs.

Goal: Establish a lightweight scoring rubric, an intake form, and a small gating checklist so PM/Eng/Design can rapidly decide: Build native SDK, provide official adapter, or document a community pattern. Deliverables:
- Prioritization rubric (weighted scores and thresholds)
- Intake form template for Solutions/Support/AE to capture signals
- Triage runbook with owners and SLAs (48h acknowledge, 10 business days initial decision)
- Example triage outcomes for 3 representative customer asks (PHP client for streaming inference; Rust production client with mTLS + token rotation; mobile JS with resumable reconnect)

Scope (what this ticket covers):
- Language coverage decision criteria (usage signals, revenue linked customers, engineering cost, runtime compatibility)
- Auth pattern requirements: token refresh flows, client credentials, SSO/OIDC federation, mTLS for private deployments, KMS/HSM considerations for Private customers
- Streaming ergonomics: reconnect/resume semantics, backpressure and chunked responses, metadata propagation, idempotency and delivery guarantees
- Non-goals: Implementation of any single SDK (those will be follow-up tickets if rubric leads to build)
source.acceptance_criteria: 1) A published rubric doc exists in Confluence and linked from the SDK intake playbook. 2) Intake form created in Zendesk/Forms and sample submitted by Support team. 3) Triage SLAs documented and agreed with Eng and Design. 4) 3 example customer requests scored and decisions recorded (adapter / build / decline). 5) Roadmap signal bucket and handoff template for follow-up tickets.
source.scoring_rubric: Scoring axes (0-10 each) with weights: customer_count (weight 1.5), revenue_impact (2.0), platform_alignment (1.0), engineering_cost_est (in dev-weeks inverted to score) (1.5), security_compliance_need (2.0), maintenance_risk (1.0). Final score = weighted sum. Thresholds: >=30 -> Build official SDK; 20-30 -> Official adapter + docs; <20 -> Guidance + community template. Example: PHP streaming client for transcription request (customer_count=6,revenue=8,platform_alignment=6,eng_cost=4,security_need=3,maintenance=5) => final=~29 => adapter + prioritized docs.
source.intake_form_template: Fields to capture: company_name, customer_tier, primary_contact, brief_use_case, estimated_monthly_requests, required_auth_modes (API key, OAuth client, SSO/OIDC, mTLS), streaming_requirements (resumable=true/false, max_chunk_ms, backpressure_behavior), required_regions, compliance_constraints (SOC2, HIPAA, FedRAMP), deadline/launch_date, business_impact_metric. Also ask for a reference app or example payloads and a contact willing to participate in early testing.
source.runbook: Triage runbook: 1) Triage owner (PM oncall) acknowledges request within 48h. 2) Triage meeting cadence: daily async scoring; weekly sync with Eng and Design for borderline items. 3) Required inputs before decision: sample traffic profile, security requirements, expected QPS, compatibility constraints (mobile/embedded). 4) Outcome templates: Build (create ENG- ticket + design spike), Adapter (create PM task + docs owner), Defer (log in backlog with signal tags). 5) Escalation path: if security_compliance_need >=8 escalate to Security for gating review. SLAs: initial decision in 10 business days, prioritized scheduling within next roadmap cycle depending on score.
example_triage_records: 2026-02-24 | Company: Veridian Health | Request: PHP SDK with streaming transcription + OAuth2 PKCE | Score: 29 | Decision: Adapter + prioritized docs; ENG follow-up: ENG-82119 (adapter spike)

2026-03-02 | Company: Atlas Financial | Request: Rust client with mTLS + token rotation for Private deployment | Score: 34 | Decision: Build official SDK for Private offering; Create ENG-82140 (rust-sdk-private) and schedule Q2 implementation

2026-03-05 | Company: SprintlyMobile | Request: JS mobile-friendly client with resumable streaming and background reconnect | Score: 22 | Decision: Provide reference implementation + add mobile-resume sample to docs; consider full SDK if pilot shows demand
comments: 2026-03-10 Aisha Kumar: Draft rubric completed and shared with PM guild; requesting Engineering and Security feedback.
2026-03-11 Ethan Park: Reviewed streaming semantics with infra team; noted that resumable streams will require runtime KV cache extension — flagged as eng_dependency ENG-81234.
2026-03-12 Maria Lopez (Security): Confirmed SSO/OIDC patterns and token rotation checklist; recommended adding 'refresh token rotation mention' and PKCE for mobile flows.
2026-03-12 Design review: Adding SDK ergonomics pattern doc link and three UI/UX checklists for quickstart examples.
2026-03-12 Action: Created intake form draft in Google Forms and posted to internal #sdk-onramp channel for pilot.

source.title: Org-scoped adaptive regression pipelines with encrypted shadow probes
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Problem: Customers running Dedicated/Private deployments require regression detection tightly coupled to model/config changes, but cannot allow customer data to leave their tenancy. We need a lightweight, org-scoped evaluation orchestration that runs privacy-preserving probes/shadow tests whenever model config or routing policies change.

Goal: Deliver an adaptive per-organization eval pipeline that: 1) triggers on model/config rollouts and routing policy changes; 2) executes inside the customer's dedicated environment (VPC/on-prem) or via client-side sampling; 3) stores only encrypted witnesses/hashes and metric aggregates in Redwood control plane; 4) provides actionable regression alerts and an opt-in automated rollback/canary flow.

Approach: Build a small runner SDK that can be deployed into Private/Dedicated tenants (container + minimal runtime) to execute curated probe suites. For hosted and Dedicated customers that cannot run code, support client-side sampling where plaintext never leaves client and only HMACed outputs and differential metrics are returned. Use per-tenant keying (KMS/HSM) to produce encrypted witnesses that allow repro requests under explicit customer authorization.

Scope: MVP scope includes tenant-runner + control-plane orchestrator, a set of templated probe suites (NLP sanity checks, hallucination detectors, tokenization drift checks), metric aggregation dashboards in Console, and an alerting rule template customers can tune.
source.acceptance_criteria:
1) Orchestrator can register tenant-runner and deploy probe job triggered by a model or config change event.
2) Probe execution for Private/Dedicated tenants runs inside tenant network (runner) and produces only encrypted witness blobs plus numeric metrics to control plane.
3) For client-side sampling mode, no raw inputs or outputs are transmitted; only HMACed/witness artifacts and metrics are sent.
4) Console shows per-tenant regression metrics and alerts within 5 minutes of job completion.
5) Automated canary rollback policy can be enabled per-tenant and tested in a non-production rollout without exposing customer PII.
6) Security review signed off and KMS integration documented.
source.success_metrics:
- 80% of eligible Private/Dedicated customers can onboard tenant-runner within 2 weeks.
- Detection time (change -> alert) median < 10 minutes for standard probe suites.
- False-positive rate on regression alerts < 5% in first 3 months.
- No incidents of customer plaintext data exfiltration; audit logs for every witness access.
- 30% of early adopters enable automated canary rollback in first quarter after launch.
comments: 2026-03-10 - Ava Chen: Kickoff notes added; scoped MVP to runner + client-side sampling. Prioritized for Q2 customer pilots.

2026-03-11 - Rajesh Patel: Initial tech-spike completed. Tenant-runner prototype (containerized) can fetch probe manifests and post encrypted witnesses. Needs KMS integration and minimal network onboarding docs.
2026-03-11 - Security: Requested threat model and KMS usage pattern for encrypted witness lifecycle. Marked security_review_required=true.
2026-03-12 - Eng (Sana Morales): Hooked change event stream to orchestrator; test job triggered successfully in staging tenant. Observability telemetry added for job timings.
2026-03-12 - PM (Ava): Demo scheduled with two pilot customers (FinRegCo, HealthApps) week of 2026-03-22. Need privacy acceptance checklist for pilots.
2026-03-12 - Blocker: Infra needs to finalize VPC onboarding automation for tenant-runner (dependency ENG-20456).

Next steps: Complete KMS/HSM integration, finalize probe suite for pilot (legal reviewed), create Console dashboard tiles and alert templates, run pilot onboarding playbook with two customers, iterate SDK ergonomics based on pilot feedback.

source.title: Account-led feature curation and scoping-sparring play
source.creator: Priya Singh
source.description: This ticket defines a repeatable play for aggregating account-sourced feature asks from Sales and Customer Success and producing a lightweight, scoping-grade output that can drive prioritization decisions. The goal is to reduce back-and-forth with Sales/CS by providing a standardized scoring and sparring session (30- to 60-minute) where PM + 1 engineer + AE validate business intent, basic technical feasibility, privacy/regulatory constraints, and an initial effort bucket. The artifact delivered is an "account bundle brief" (single-page) and a normalized scoring row in the enterprise intake board.

source.title: PRD: Adversarial prompt probes, cohorted signal attenuation, and circuit-breaker rollback for Optimize
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Goal: Add an automated layer to Optimize that (1) generates adversarial probe prompts to stress test model variants, (2) groups signals into stable cohorts with attenuation for noisy indicators, and (3) enforces a two-tier circuit-breaker rollback choreography (soft-stop then hard-stop) tied to composite regression scoring. This PRD specifies requirements, acceptance criteria, and rollout steps for the 2026-Q2 Optimize quality-safety initiative.

Background: Current Optimize evals rely on static promptsets and fixed thresholds that underreact to adversarial or distribution-shift events. Customers running production can experience silent regressions in small but important cohorts (e.g., legal, finance, medical). We need a system that proactively finds brittle prompts, weights cohort signals by reliability, and automates safe traffic decay/rollback when multiple signals align.

Non-goals: This ticket does not change model training, on-the-fly model editing, or billing metrics. It does not implement new UI visualizations beyond concise alert cards and a rollback button (follow-on design work is separate).
source.proposed_solution: 1) Adversarial probe generator: use existing prompt corpus + mutation heuristics (template swaps, negation injection, chained-rewriting) to synthesize ~2k adversarial probes per candidate model variant; allow manual seed lists. 2) Cohort formation: categorize prompt probes into cohorts by intent, sensitivity, and frequency (e.g., legal-sensitive, numeric-extraction, multi-turn-state). Attach reliability score per cohort computed from historical variance and sample counts. 3) Signal attenuation: apply a decay factor to high-variance cohorts to reduce false positives; persistent regressions (>=3 consecutive evaluations) escalate attenuation weight upward. 4) Composite regression score: weighted sum of standardized deltas across primary metrics (accuracy/f1), safety violation rate, hallucination proxies, latency and cost delta. 5) Circuit-breaker choreography: soft-stop reduces traffic to new variant by 50% and triggers an alert when composite score exceeds 0.06 OR any cohort sees >5% absolute drop in primary metric over 2 windows; hard-stop rolls back to baseline if composite score exceeds 0.12 OR any safety cohort exceeds 1% absolute increase in safety violations. 6) A/B sizing: dynamic sequential test with alpha=0.01, beta=0.2, minimum detectable effect 3% absolute for primary metric, with early stopping rules. 7) Integrations: GitHub PR links for rollout automation, Console alert card for on-call, and audit log entries for Private customers.
acceptance_criteria: Adversarial probe generation: system can produce >=1500 unique adversarial probes for five representative models within a single run and persist probe provenance to storage (logs/URL to dataset).
Cohort reliability: cohorts have computed reliability score in [0,1] and attenuation is applied such that high-variance cohorts reduce their weight by at least 30% for a single anomalous window.
Composite score thresholds enforced: soft-stop triggers at composite >= 0.06; hard-stop at composite >= 0.12. Thresholds configurable via Console feature flags.
A/B detection: A/B framework detects a 3% absolute change in primary metric with >=80% power within the sample budget and supports early stopping when sequential p-value crosses alpha (0.01).

Rollback behavior: soft-stop traffic decay executes within 60s of trigger; hard-stop rollback completes and returns traffic to baseline routing within 5 minutes. Events are recorded to audit logs with a user-visible reason and linking PR/rollback runbook.
Safety guarantee: any single safety cohort crossing +1.0 percentage point in safety violation rate triggers immediate hard-stop (manual override preserved).

Observability: Dashboard shows composite score, per-cohort deltas, probe provenance links, and a timeline of attenuation/stop events. Alerts post to on-call and create an incident stub with relevant artifacts.
scope_changes: Initial spike included UI redesign of detailed cohort pages; deprioritized to Alert card + details link to keep scope manageable.
Will not include custom training data ingestion for probe generation in phase 1; only use existing prompt corpora and manual seed lists.
Hard-stop automation limited to hosted & dedicated modes in phase 1; Private mode will require admin opt-in and longer audit windows.
rollout_plan: Phase 0 (canary): run adversarial probe generator and cohort scoring in dry-run against staging models; verify composite score distribution (2 weeks).
Phase 1 (limited canary): enable soft-stop automation for 1% of Optimize traffic with Console alerts and no automatic hard-stop; collect metrics and on-call feedback (2 weeks).
Phase 2 (gradual expand): enable soft-stop at 10% then 50% with automatic hard-stop gated behind Safety flag for hosted/dedicated customers (3 weeks), include runbook link and incident automation.
Phase 3 (GA): enable full choreography with configurable thresholds and Console controls; provide rollout notes and customer comms (1 week).
metrics_and_signals: Primary metric: cohort-level correctness (task-dependent; normalized delta used in composite score).
Safety metric: safety-violation rate (policy hits per 1k responses).
Cost metric: per-request token cost delta normalized to baseline throughput.
Performance: p95 latency delta for inference path and routing fallback counts.
Probe coverage: unique probe count, collision rate, provenance link to generation run.
Composite regression score: standardized weighted sum exposed in Console (0-1 scale).

2026-03-11 - Priya Nandakumar (Engineering): Reviewed. Need telemetry hooks for KV cache effects; soft-stop must also flush relevant KV caches to avoid stale context. Request an eng spike for cost estimate (ENG-44789).

2026-03-11 - Mateo Ruiz: Will take ownership of implementation. Plan to split work into two epics: 1) probe generation & cohort scoring, 2) automation & routing. Estimate 8 points for MVP. Will coordinate with infra on audit logs.

2026-03-12 - Dara Li (Design): Alert card mockups uploaded to Drive: https://drive.redwood.example/designs/optimize-circuit-breaker. Suggest including a one-click 'pause automation' button for on-call safety.
2026-03-12 - Legal (comment): No new user data flows beyond existing storage; confirm retention policy for generated probes (recommend 90 days).

title: PRD: Adversarial prompt probes, cohorted signal attenuation, and circuit-breaker rollback for Optimize
preview:
Goal: Add an automated layer to Optimize that (1) generates adversarial probe prompts to stress test model variants, (2) groups signals into stable cohorts with attenuation for noisy indicators, and (3) enforces a two-tier circuit-breaker rollback choreography (soft-stop then hard-stop) tied to composite regression scoring. This PRD specifies requirements, acceptance criteria, and rollout steps for the 2026-Q2 Optimize quality-safety initiative.

Background: Current Optimize evals rely on static promptsets and fixed thresholds that underreact to adversarial or distribution-shift events. Customers running production can experience silent regressions in small but important cohorts (e.g., legal, finance, medical). We need a system that proactively finds brittle prompts, weights cohort signals by reliability, and automates safe traffic decay/rollback when multiple signals align.

Non-goals: This ticket does not change model training, on-the-fly model editing, or billing metrics. It does not implement new UI visualizations beyond concise alert cards and a rollback button (follow-on design work is separate).

source.title: Establish review runbook and handshake protocol for cross-team PRD approvals
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.body: Background: Recent PRDs for Dedicated autoscale and Private upgrade experienced multi-week delays due to unclear reviewer responsibilities, missing legal checklist items, and inconsistent handoffs between PM, Design, and Engineering. Goal: define a lightweight, repeatable runbook and ’handshake’ protocol that standardizes how PRDs move through review and sign-off with SLAs, templates, and an escalation path. Deliverables include a Confluence runbook, a PRD template with section-level owners and checkboxes, a Linear review workflow pattern, and a pilot with two upcoming PRDs (Dedicated autoscale and Private upgrades).

Defined SLAs for initial review (48 hours) and final sign-off (5 business days) documented and agreed by stakeholders

source.title: Coordinated Sunset Workflow: Automated Notices, Pin Recommendations, and Rollback Paths for Legacy Model Versions
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Goal: Define and deliver an automated, low-friction sunset workflow for aging model versions that combines proactive customer notices, in-console pin recommendations, published performance/compatibility profiles, and safe rollback paths.

Background: We operate many model variants and publish performance profiles for each. Customers sometimes rely on older weights or older quantized builds. When we must retire a version (weight or tuning), ad-hoc notices and manual support create churn and escalations. This initiative packages the end-to-end flow so we can retire versions with predictable customer outcomes and minimal support load.

Key deliverables: 1) Sunset timeline template with notice windows (early warning, last-call, final shutdown) and per-tier variations (self-serve, enterprise SLA). 2) In-console "Pin Recommendation" card: automatically suggests compatible stable variants and a one-click pin for customers at risk. 3) Automated migration guides: pre-rendered change logs, prompt diff examples, and per-language SDK snippets to reduce integration friction. 4) Performance & compatibility publishing: ensure performance profile pages for replacement variants are up-to-date and linked from notices. 5) Telemetry-driven targeting: identify customers still using retiring versions and surface personalized notices + migration priority. 6) Safe rollback playbook: automated rollback triggers, customer-visible downgrade protection windows, and ops runbook links.

Workflow summary: - T-90 days: Register retirement in lifecycle dashboard; create migration guide draft and compatibility matrix. - T-60 days: Publish early-warning email + in-console banner; begin telemetry to measure active usage. - T-30 days: Pin Recommendation roll-out in console (A/B for 10% customers initially); provide one-click SDK deprecation warnings and sample migration PRs. - T-7 days: Last-call notice, create staged capacity fences for automatic fallbacks. - T-0: Disable serving variant; route traffic to chosen fallback if customer has not pinned and fallback is permitted; generate incident/ops ticket for manual assists.

Acceptance criteria: - Customers running retiring versions receive a clear notice in console and email at T-60 and T-30, with links to migration guide. - Pin Recommendation card shows correct compatible variant and estimated impact on latency and cost using published performance profiles. - At least 90% of identified customers have either pinned a replacement or engaged support within the window for targeted retirements. - Rollback procedure validated in a canary test and documented in runbook.

Links and artifacts: - Migration guide template: https://confluence.redwood.ai/model-lifecycle/migration-template - Pin card mockups: https://drive.redwood.ai/design/pin-card-mockups - Telemetry dashboard: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/model-lifecycle/retirement-usage

Open questions: - Should we allow automatic silent fallback for P0 customers or require explicit opt-in? - How to compute estimated cost/latency delta in Pin Recommendation when hardware differs across regions?

Next steps: Align on policy for automated fallback (PM + Legal), finalize Pin Recommendation copy and telemetry signals, kick off ENG dependency tickets to implement console UI and automated email orchestration.

Pin Recommendation card shows correct compatible variant and estimated impact on latency and cost using published performance profiles
At least 90% of identified customers have either pinned a replacement or engaged support within the window for targeted retirements
Rollback procedure validated in a canary test and documented in runbook

source.title: Coordinate cross-team acceptance matrix for Private VPC upgrade toggle
source.creator: Luca Martinez
source.description: PRD: Add a user-facing toggle in the Redwood Console to enable the Private VPC upgrade path (v2 networking + optional KMS-based envelope encryption). This ticket coordinates cross-team acceptance criteria and sign-offs required before the toggle ships: engineering readiness for migration hooks and rollout flags; design acceptance for console UX, error states, and migration prompts; legal/compliance confirmation for data residency and export control language. Scope clarifications added after initial PRD: KMS integration will be optional for first wave; migration rollback path must be automated; customers with Reserved Dedicated instances are out of scope for this rollout. Goal: produce a single acceptance matrix and checklist that individual teams can sign off against to unblock release-2026.03.

source.title: Reservation handshake runbook and scaling safety net
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Create an end-to-end runbook and negotiation flow for dedicated capacity reservations that: 1) standardizes the reservation handshake between Sales/CS/Provisioning, 2) encodes throughput guarantees into deployable plans, and 3) provides a safety net of autoscale and burst mechanisms to protect customer SLAs while preserving cluster stability and cost controls. This artifact should be the canonical source for onboarding engineers, SREs, and account teams when a customer requests committed throughput or reserved GPU pools.
source.background: We repeatedly see ad-hoc reservation requests that lack consistent sizing inputs (e.g., request for 'X GPUs' without workload characteristics), resulting in overprovisioning or SLA misses. Past playbooks are fragmented across Confluence pages and handoffs are error-prone: infra teams manually create fleets, SRE applies naive autoscale rules, and there is no standard probation window or burst policy. This ticket consolidates learnings from incident INC-2025-11 (late scale causing 20% degraded p95) and PM intake analytics showing 32% revision rate on initial reservations.
source.acceptance_criteria:
1) Provide a documented reservation form (fields + validation) that captures traffic patterns, peak concurrency, latency SLOs, model family, token/embedding mix, and expected daily/weekly seasonality.
2) Finalized handshake flow with clear responsibilities (Sales -> PM -> Provisioning -> SRE) and automatable handoff (GitHub issue template + Confluence checklist).
3) Implement three autoscaling policy templates (steady-state, burstable, high-variance) with concrete parameters (min/max node counts, target_utilization, burst_pool credits, cooldown windows).
4) Safety net defined: probation window (14 days), elastic burst credits, and a fallback routing policy to compatible lower-cost model variants.
5) Runbook includes monitoring dashboard links, playbook run steps for escalation, and two runbook drills recorded in Ops calendar.
6) Confirm end-to-end test in staging that simulates a 3x traffic spike with rollback behavior validated in < 5 minutes.
source.decision_log:
- 2026-02-20: Agreed to use a 14-day probation window (trade-off: longer window provides stability but delays final measurement).
- 2026-02-25: Chose three autoscale templates instead of per-customer bespoke rules to reduce support burden.
- 2026-03-02: Burst credits will be meterable and limited by a chargeback queue; finance prefers credit usage tracked daily rather than minute-granular billing.
- 2026-03-08: Default fallback is model-variant routing (e.g., fp16->int8 hybrid) rather than cross-region failover to avoid cold-start penalties.
source.implementation_notes:
- Reservation form: implement as a guarded step in the onboarding UI and as a GitHub issue template for manual intake.
- Autoscale templates: translate into fleet config YAML in infra repo (see example template in repo PR).
- Metrics: instrument reservations with tags reservation_id and commitment_tier; emit reservation-specific cost and p99 latency metrics.
- CI: add a staged test harness that replays customer-shaped traffic (workload generator at infra/test/playbooks).
- Ops: add runbook entries to PagerDuty escalation path for reservation breaches; include sample Slack cadence for notification.
source.rollout_plan:
Phase 1 (2 weeks): final review of runbook and reservation form; implement GitHub intake template; pilot with two Customer Success-managed accounts.
Phase 2 (3 weeks): land autoscale templates and instrumentation; run two drills and adjust burst_pool sizing.
Phase 3 (2 weeks): automate handoff from Sales to Provisioning via the intake endpoint; train GTM and SRE on the runbook; announce to customers with dedicated onboarding guide.
source.risks_and_mitigation:
Risk: Autoscale templates mis-sized -> can either under-allocate (SLA breach) or over-allocate (customer cost). Mitigation: conservative default with probation telemetry + weekly auto-tuning recommendations from Optimize.
Risk: Burst credits abused by noisy tenants. Mitigation: per-reservation credit caps, chargeback reporting, and automatic throttling policy after credit depletion.
Risk: Complexity for small customers. Mitigation: provide a simplified 'starter reservation' mode with one-click defaults.
comments: 2026-02-16 - Ava Chen: Created initial RFC after feedback from SRE on INC-2025-11. Added prototype reservation schema and proposed autoscale templates.
2026-02-22 - Priya Natarajan (SRE): Reviewed autoscale template defaults; suggested lowering target_utilization for high-variance workloads and adding a longer cooldown for scale-downs.
2026-02-28 - Diego Martinez: Pulled draft into infra repo as PR #4123; working with infra to ensure config schema compatibility with cluster-autoscaler v3.
2026-03-05 - Maya Singh (Sales Eng): Requested additional fields on form to capture contractual billing terms and preferred maintenance windows.
2026-03-09 - Finance (Liam O'Connor): Confirmed preference for daily credit metering; asked for a report schema for monthly reconciliation.
2026-03-10 - Ava Chen: Updated acceptance criteria to require staging smoke tests and two runbook drills; queued training session for 2026-03-22.
2026-03-10 - Diego Martinez: Assigned ENG-2309 to implement metric tagging and reservation-specific dashboards.
2026-03-10 - Ava Chen: Note: we will not enable automatic cross-region fallback in Phase 1 to avoid multi-region config complexity.
source.next_steps: 1) Finalize reservation form fields and validation (owner: PM). 2) Land infra PR for autoscale templates (owner: ENG). 3) Add reservation tags to telemetry pipeline and create dashboard (owner: SRE). 4) Pilot with two low-risk customers and collect telemetry for two weeks.
source.notes: Keep this runbook iterative: expect 2-3 revisions after the pilot based on observed burst patterns. Ensure legal signs-off on SLA language before surfacing throughput guarantees in contracts.

source.title: Quantile Warranty & Autoscale Coordination for Dedicated Serve Capacity
source.creator: Aisha Kapoor
source.description:
Problem statement: enterprise customers need clear, measurable guarantees for dedicated GPU pools. Current SLA drafts use single-point targets (e.g., tokens/min) and simple credit tables that don't reflect bursty real-world traffic or staggered degradation. This ticket defines a quantile-driven warranty (p95/p99 windows + AUC degradation) and ties it to autoscaler interlocks, customer notices, and contract crediting language.

Scope:
- Define measurable SLA metrics (quantiles, sustained shortfalls, AUC-based crediting).
- Specify autoscaler guardrails and interlock logic to avoid oscillation between autoscale and remediation credits.
- Draft customer-facing contract clauses (notice windows, remediation steps, credit computation appendix).
- Produce monitoring dashboards and alerting thresholds needed to support legal measurement.

Out of scope: per-tenant billing/credits plumbing and final legal sign-off (legal will review after product draft).
source.acceptance_criteria:
1) A quantile-first SLA spec document (p95/p99 and 1/5/15-minute sustained windows) reviewed by runtime and SRE.
2) Autoscale interlock design that prevents double-counting of capacity events and provides a clear fallback path.
3) Example credit calculation spreadsheet and two worked examples (short spike vs prolonged degradation).
4) Customer-facing SLA language draft suitable for inclusion in Dedicated contracts.
5) Dashboard mocks showing measurement points and alerting playbook.

All acceptance criteria must include owner sign-off from Runtime ENG, SRE, and Legal.
source.implementation_notes:
High-level approach: measure per-tenant serve rate as a distribution over 1m/5m/15m windows. Compute p95 and p99 observed serve-rates over rolling 30-day windows. For crediting, integrate shortfall area (AUC) below committed quantile curve over a 30-day window to compute proportional credit. Autoscaler interlock: when autoscaler executes a scale-up/scale-down action, mark a 10-minute grace window where subsequent shortfalls are classified as 'autoscale in-flight' and excluded from immediate customer credits; persistent shortfalls beyond two autoscale cycles trigger remediation credits.

Trade-offs considered: AUC-based credits better reflect economic harm vs rigid minute-by-minute lapses, but are more complex to explain to customers. We will include worked examples and a per-customer compute of credits to increase transparency.

Instrumentation changes required: per-tenant serve counters, event tagging for autoscale actions, rolling-window quantile aggregator, and a daily reconcile job for credit computation.
comments: 2026-02-13 - Aisha K: Kickoff meeting notes posted. Runtime asked for examples of traffic profiles. Action: ENG to capture 3 anonymized RPS traces by 2/20.
2026-02-20 - Marcus L: Uploaded aggregated RPS traces (spike, diurnal, heavy-tail). Runtime suggests p99 measurement should be computed on trimmed windows to ignore outlier telemetry artifacts.

2026-02-25 - SRE (Jia Chen): Proposed autoscaler interlock: 10-minute in-flight window + exponential backoff to avoid oscillation. Concern: 10 minutes may be too short for multi-region failovers. Marked for follow-up with infra team.

2026-03-02 - Legal (R. Patel): Draft contract paragraphs reviewed. Requested a simpler plain-language summary for customer-facing SLA, and explicit definition of 'service credit'. Legal asked for a 'no-fault' clause for provider-mitigated capacity changes (e.g., AZ maintenance).

2026-03-05 - PM review: Added AUC credit worked example for a 3-hour partial outage vs a 1-hour full outage. Decision: AUC credits will yield the same or higher credit than per-minute worst-case for most realistic traffic shapes.
2026-03-08 - Design (Evan Morales): Dashboard mockups uploaded showing p95/p99 bands and AUC shortfall shaded. Requested time-series annotations for autoscale events and incident links.
2026-03-10 - Marcus L: Draft autoscaler interlock design submitted for ENG review (ENG-5098). Next: finalize crediting spreadsheet and route to Legal for final wording by 3/24.
source.customer_communication: Planned customer-facing one-pager that explains: (a) what the quantile-based warranty measures, (b) how credits are calculated with simple worked examples, (c) notification timelines and SLA portal references. Draft to be reviewed by Customer Success and Legal before release.
contract_language_snippets: Committed Serve Warranty: Provider guarantees that, over each 30-day measurement period, observed per-tenant serve rates will meet or exceed the committed p95 serve-rate for at least 99% of the time. Shortfalls will be measured as the integrated shortfall area below the committed quantile curve and credited as set out in the Crediting Appendix.

Notice & Remediation: Customer will be notified within two business hours of detecting a sustained shortfall beyond the p99 threshold. Provider will execute configured remediation (autoscale or fallback) and provide a root-cause summary within 72 hours. Credits are applied after reconciling the measurement windows and confirmed not to be caused by Customer misconfiguration.

Autoscale Interlock: Scale actions (up/down) taken by Provider within the measurement window will be annotated. Shortfalls occurring during an autoscale in-flight window (10 minutes post-action) are not eligible for automatic credits; persistent shortfalls beyond two consecutive autoscale cycles are eligible for crediting as defined in the Crediting Appendix.

source.title: Inference Moat Metric: quantifying serving advantage & operational readiness
source.creator: Aisha Raman
source.description: Problem: Sales, product and exec teams lack a single, quantitative view of Redwood's 'inference moat' — the concrete serving, routing, and ops capabilities buyers cite when choosing us over other providers. Existing artifacts are fragmented (benchmarks, PRDs, ad-hoc research). This ticket will synthesize a repeatable metric ("Inference Moat Score") and an operations-ready signal set that can be surfaced in Console and exported to GTM. The goal is to turn qualitative win-loss themes into measurable signals that drive roadmap prioritization, pricing, and competitive messaging.
source.proposal_summary: Define a ~10-dimension composite score combining internal telemetry (latency P95, KV cache hit, fallback success rate, real-time autoscale time), product features (private VPC readiness, dedicated capacity lead time, model-variant coverage), market signals (public model availability churn, quantization support), and procurement friction (contract complexity, compliance steps). Build a prototype dashboard in Console (read-only) and a CSV export for Sales. Pilot with 3 enterprise accounts and 1 internal GTM dossier. Deliverables: scoring rubric, data pipelines, dashboard proto, playbook for GTM usage and engineering flags to prioritize mitigations.
acceptance_criteria: Scorecard spec: formal definition for each dimension, weight, expected value ranges, and normalization method
Data ingestion: ETL queries or metrics definition for each signal (Console metrics, infra telemetry, procurement ticket tags) with daily refresh
Prototype dashboard: Console view showing global leaderboard, per-customer score, and top-3 contributing signals
Export & alerts: CSV export endpoint and one alert rule (score delta > 15% week-over-week) wired to GTM slack channel
Pilot feedback: documented feedback from 3 customers and 2 AE interviews and iteration plan
tasks: PM: finalize dimensions & weighting (owners: PM + Research)
Research: run correlation analysis between historical loss reasons and candidate signals
Eng (Data): implement metrics queries and daily ETL to metrics store
Eng (Console): mock dashboard + visual design handoff
Design: craft visualization and explanatory microcopy for score components
GTM: prepare sales one-pager and 2 demo scripts for pilots
Pilot: onboard 3 enterprise customers and collect structured feedback
Iterate: update weights and surface remediation tickets for high-leverage deficiencies
comments: Aisha Raman (2026-03-02): Drafted initial dimensions after interviewing 5 AE/SEs — included their top win/loss phrases.
Mateo Alvarez (2026-03-04): Need to ensure private deployment signals (KMS/HSM readiness) are represented separately — enterprise buyers weight this heavily.
Priya Kaur (Research, 2026-03-06): Preliminary correlation shows cache hit rate and fallback success predict deal closure better than raw P95 under 10s latency buckets.
Elena Park (Design, 2026-03-08): Will provide dashboard mockups by 2026-03-12; recommend using small-multiples to show signal trends over time.
updates: 2026-03-05: Research completed correlation analysis; removed redundant dimension 'token-throughput' and replaced with 'fallback success rate'.
2026-03-09: Data team validated availability of required metrics; ETL window estimated at 4 hours daily.
2026-03-10: Console team accepted read-only dashboard scope; charting lib decision: use existing timeseries viz component.
source.notes_for_sales: Provide a short narrative and one-pager that maps score ranges to deal motions (e.g., 80+ means low friction for dedicated/private; 50-80 indicates targeted mitigations; <50 requires technical deep-dive). Include 2 demo scripts showing how to use the score in pre-sales calls.
source.risk_and_mitigation: Risk: signals are noisy and may misclassify accounts, leading to GTM mistrust. Mitigation: conservative rollout with manual review for first 6 pilots, clear caveats in one-pager, and feedback loop to adjust weights.
open_questions: What is the canonical customer identifier for joining procurement tickets to telemetry?
Should external public model availability signals be pulled from third-party provider feeds or seeded manually?
Do we expose raw component scores to customers or only the composite?
