source.title: Privilege shadowing, SSO assertion hardening, and KMS audit sign-off
source.creator: Aisha Patel
source.description: Clarify requirements, scope changes, and acceptance criteria for three tightly-coupled security controls for Private/VPC deployments: (1) privilege shadowing (temporary operator impersonation with auditable traces), (2) SSO/SAML assertion hardening and federation verification, and (3) KMS/HSM-integrated audit evidence and key-escrow operational controls. This ticket consolidates ambiguous PRD language, captures engineering constraints, and presents a sign-off checklist for security, legal, and product owners.
source.scope:
- Implement 'privilege shadowing' flows where operators can assume customer-adjacent roles for debugging with ephemeral credentials and full, tamper-evident audit logs.
- Define SAML assertion validation matrix (audience, audience restriction, issuer signing algorithm, assertion expiry skew, and replay detection).
- Ensure KMS/HSM key use is logged: all encrypt/decrypt/sign requests produce structured audit events exportable to customer SIEM.
- Provide ops runbooks for key rotation, emergency break-glass, and evidence export for audits.
source.out_of_scope:
- Changes to hosted API tenancy model.
- Multi-tenant shard-level RBAC re-architecture.
- New KMS vendor onboarding beyond current supported providers (AWS KMS, GCP KMS, HashiCorp Vault HSM).
requirements: Privilege shadowing must generate a single correlation_id that ties the impersonation request, ephemeral credential issuance, all downstream API calls, and the operator's real identity in the audit trail.
Ephemeral credentials issued for privilege shadowing expire within a configurable window (default 15 min) and are revocable via control plane immediately.

SAML assertions accepted by the control plane must enforce assertion expiry < 5 minutes, audience restriction matching the tenant SP URI, and signature verification against a rotating metadata endpoint.
Replay protection: control plane rejects duplicate assertion IDs within a configurable replay window (default 10m) and emits explicit 'saml.replay_detected' audit events.

All KMS/HSM operations relevant to customer keying (wrap/unwrap, sign, decrypt) must emit structured audit events containing: operation, key_id (obfuscated in logs per policy), caller_identity, timestamp, request_id, and outcome.
Audit retention defaults: 90 days in control plane hot storage; must support export to customer-managed storage (S3/GCS) with lifecycle policies for 7 years.
Break-glass flow: documented emergency escalation with dual-operator approval or time-limited one-operator override with mandatory post-incident attestation.
RBAC mapping: map SAML groups to least-privilege built-in roles; support custom role bindings but deny elevation without explicit approval and audit evidence.
Console UI must surface a tamper-evidence digest (SHA256) for exported audit bundles and allow customers to verify bundle integrity.
Operational metrics: emit per-tenant counts for shadowing sessions, failed assertion verifications, KMS errors, and export success/failure rates to observability dashboards.
Provide e2e test harness and an acceptance checklist for security sign-off including reproducible test vectors for SAML assertions and KMS operations.
acceptance_criteria: Given a tenant and operator with appropriate permissions, when an operator initiates privilege shadowing, then the system issues ephemeral credentials tied to a correlation_id and starts an auditable session that records all downstream calls.
Given a forged or replayed SAML assertion, the control plane must reject the request and emit an auditable rejection event with reason code.
Given a KMS encrypt/decrypt request via private deploy, the system emits structured audit logs which are present in the customer's audit export and contain required fields per the requirements list.

Given an emergency break-glass activation, the system enforces the configured approval workflow, records rationale, and surfaces the session in the audit export with post-incident attestation metadata.
Audit export integrity verification: exported bundle contains SHA256 digest and verification step passes using the provided console UI or CLI tool.
Security review sign-off: Security Engineering, Legal, and Customer Ops have reviewed and provided explicit sign-off lines in the checklist.
test_scenarios: Happy path: operator with 'support:impersonate' starts shadowing; create workload requests against model endpoints; verify correlation_id in audit log ties operator to each request and that ephemeral creds expire after 15m.
SAML negative test: send a valid assertion but altered audience; expect 401-like rejection, log event 'saml.audience_mismatch', and no session created.
SAML replay test: replay same assertion twice within 10m, expect second attempt rejected with 'saml.replay_detected'.
KMS HSM path: perform sign operation using customer HSM; verify audit event emitted and available in export; run integrity verification on exported bundle.
Break-glass test: trigger emergency override path with single operator (when enabled for test tenant); validate attestation prompt and that post-incident attestation is required before closure.
Retention/export test: generate 1000 audit events, trigger export to S3 bucket, verify manifest, digest, and lifecycle policy attachment.
checklist: Product: PRD updated with clear flows and UI wireframes (link in artifacts).
Security: Threat model updated and reviewed (CSP, replay, key compromise scenarios).
Engineering: Impl plan + rollout canary strategy documented; automated e2e tests green in CI.
Legal: Reviewed retention and export policies for compliance with customer regulatory regimes.
Customer Ops: Runbook for onboarding, emergency rotation, and evidence export validated with a pilot customer.
Support: Playbook for handling suspected misuse of shadowing with escalation contacts.
Sign-offs: Security Engineering (name & date), Legal (name & date), Product (name & date), Engineering Lead (name & date).
comments: 2026-02-16 - Aisha Patel: Drafted ticket after cross-functional sync. Noted ambiguity in PRD around replay window and retention durations.
2026-02-23 - Mateo Alvarez: Engineering feasibility review completed; HSM vendor integration requires small schema addition to audit event but no protocol change.
2026-02-28 - Security Eng (Lin Chen): Requested explicit test vectors for SAML assertion expiry and replay; added requirement for metadata rotation window to be <= 24 hours.
2026-03-04 - Legal (Marina Cortez): Confirmed 7-year export retention is acceptable for high-reg compliance customers; asked to codify opt-in customer consent flow.
2026-03-09 - Mateo Alvarez: Implementation PR linked; initial canary deployment scheduled for 2026-03-15 in us-west-2 dedicated pools.
2026-03-10 - Aisha Patel: Ready for stakeholder sign-off review; security checklist items marked for final validation.

source.title: Emergent LLM Pulse: Rapid Recon & Procurement Trigger Playbook
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Create a repeatable, lightweight process (Emergent LLM Pulse) for rapidly identifying promising new open / vendor LLMs, scoring them for deployment fit, and emitting automated procurement/trial triggers for Redwood teams. The output will be a taxonomy, telemetry-driven alert rules, a one-page procurement decision canvas, and a prototype dashboard that surfaces top emergent models and suggested fallback/rollout actions.
source.background: The model landscape is accelerating: new model variants, quant formats, and licensing events show up weekly. Sales and platform teams need early warning and an operational decision path so we can evaluate and onboard candidate models without derailing SRE/infra cycles. Existing model-scout docs are deep but slow; this ticket is to operationalize a fast loop for signal -> triage -> procurement trigger.
goals: Define 8 concise signal dimensions (latency, quantization readiness, license friction, community traction, bench eval delta, hardware affinity, stability incidents, enterprise affinity)
Design scoring rubric that maps signals to action buckets: Observe, Probe (lab), Field Trial, Procurement (committed capacity)
Deliver a 1-page procurement decision canvas and sample alert rules for the model catalog/console
Prototype a lightweight emergent-model dashboard (mock + acceptance criteria) and handoff to eng for telemetry alerts
source.scope: Signal definitions, scoring rubric, procurement canvas, alert rule templates, dashboard mockups, and a short runbook for ops handoff. Does not include implementing infra-level autoscaling or purchasing capacity; those are follow-ups.
source.out_of_scope: Full model onboarding, legal contract negotiation, Dedicated capacity provisioning, long-form benchmarking reports.
acceptance_criteria: Taxonomy with clear definitions for each signal dimension and data source for each signal
Scoring rubric validated with 3 historical case studies (e.g., recent open-model emergence) showing expected bucket assignment
Procurement decision canvas reviewed and approved by Legal, SRE lead, and Head of Sales
Dashboard mock (Figma) with top-5 emergent-model widgets and two example alert rules documented
Runbook outlining who runs the probe (PM/R&D/Solutions), expected SLA for initial probe (7 days), and escalation path
tasks: Run discovery interviews: Sales (2), Solutions Eng (2), SRE (1), Legal (1) — capture hard constraints
Inventory current data sources: model-repo feeds, Hugging Face activity, GitHub stars, Discord/Reddit spikes, benchmark lab outputs, third-party vendor bulletins
Draft signal taxonomy and map to data sources
Create scoring rubric and map thresholds to action buckets
Produce procurement decision canvas (one-pager) and circulate for review
Design dashboard mock in Figma and document two alert templates (e.g., high community traction + permissive license -> Probe; sudden license change on core model -> Observe + Legal alert)
Validate rubric on 3 historical events and capture learnings
Prepare short handoff package for Eng (alert spec, sample queries, owner list, runbook)
comments: 2026-03-01 - Ava Chen: Kickoff w/ Sales + SRE scheduled for 2026-03-03. Goal: align on what triggers a procurement conversation vs. a lab probe.

2026-03-03 - Priya Kapoor: Discovery notes: Sales wants a 'low-friction view' of enterprise-ready traits; SRE emphasized hardware affinity (A100 vs. H100) and quant expectations. Legal flagged GPL-compatible licenses as medium friction.

2026-03-05 - Diego Morales (Eng): Feasibility: we can source Hugging Face activity feeds + GH stars + release cadence hooks within 2 weeks. Real-time latency signals will need integration with benchmark lab (follow-up ticket).

2026-03-08 - Priya Kapoor: Draft taxonomy circulated. Key debate: whether to weight community traction higher than license permissiveness for Probe bucket. Suggest moderate weight to license for Procurement triggers.

2026-03-10 - Legal (Maria Velasquez): Quick review: procurement canvas must include a mandatory legal checkpoint for any change in license or model provenance before field trials. Adding as an acceptance criterion.
decisions: Keep the initial process lightweight: Alert -> 7-day Probe -> Decision. Avoid automatic procurement without Legal signoff.
Signal weighting will initially be heuristic (0-10 per signal) and stored in a CSV/Google Sheet for iteration; plan to instrument in the model catalog later.
Dashboard will be a read-only prototype first; alerts will be delivered to #pm-model-scouting and to a dedicated PagerDuty rotation only for high-severity license/regulatory events.
next_steps: By 2026-03-15: Complete discovery interviews and consolidate notes
By 2026-03-22: Publish taxonomy + scoring rubric draft and validate against 3 historical case studies
By 2026-03-29: Finalize procurement canvas and Figma dashboard mock and schedule Eng handoff
By 2026-04-05: Create follow-up tickets for implementation (alert infra, benchmark integration, legal signoff automation)

source.title: Cross-Product Launch Calendar: Dynamic Release Train Orchestrator
source.creator: Marco Chen
source.description:
Objective: Build a Dynamic Release Train Orchestrator (DRTO) that provides a single authoritative launch calendar and automated conflict-resolution layer for runtime, console, and API releases. DRTO should allow teams to reserve launch slots, attach canary windows, declare blackout windows and capacity reservations, and automatically compute safe stagger policies and fallback lanes when conflicts are detected.

Background: Launch friction has been a recurring source of latency and outages when multiple product teams schedule large runtime or console changes in overlapping windows. The current playbooks are manual (spreadsheets + Slack) and cause avoidable collisions, overbooked GPU capacity, and late-stage rollbacks. This initiative aims to reduce schedule conflicts, accelerate cross-team coordination, and provide automated signals for go/no-go decisions.

Scope (MVP):
- Central calendar service integrated into Redwood Console with read/write reservations for runtime, console, API releases.
- Basic conflict detection and advisory recommendations (suggest alternative slots, show capacity impact).
- Canary/cohort tagging and automatic canary window insertion for model-impacting runtime changes.
- Exportable cutover checklist generator and notification hooks for oncall and stakeholders.
- Integrations: GitHub PR metadata parsing (release tag), Jira/Linear link attachment, and a lightweight API to query capacity reservations.

Out of scope (MVP):
- Enforced scheduling locks that block deployments (will be considered after advisory pilot).
- Deep autoscaling automation tied to DRTO decisions (future).

Key stakeholders:
- Product: Priya Natarajan (owner)
- Runtime Engineering: Elena Park (lead)
- Console UX: Jonah Rivera (design lead)
- Platform Reliability: Samir Gupta (SRE review)
- Growth/Customer Ops: Hannah Lee (customer comms)

Design decisions and trade-offs:
- Advisory vs Enforced mode: MVP will be advisory only to avoid blocking teams during early rollout; enforcement can be added if advisory outcomes are proven reliable.
- Centralized calendar: pros = single source of truth; cons = potential bottleneck and increased coordination overhead. We'll mitigate with well-defined owner roles and delegated sub-calendars for large orgs.
- Automated slot recommendations will favor minimal model-impact windows for runtime upgrades (nighttime in region, low-traffic cohorts) but allow overrides with documented risk acceptance.

Metrics / Success criteria:
- Reduce schedule collisions (two+ major changes in overlapping capacity-sensitive windows) by 80% in first quarter after pilot.
- Decrease late-stage rollbacks attributable to scheduling conflicts by 50%.
- Mean time to resolve calendar conflicts (manual overrides reduced) under 24 hours.
- Adoption: 60% of runtime/console release owners use DRTO advisory for new releases within 3 months of pilot.

Operational plan and rollout:
1) Week 0-4: Implement calendar backend, reservation schema, and conflict detection engine.
2) Week 5-8: Console UI integration (read/write) + GitHub/Linear linking and notifications.
3) Week 9-10: Pilot with runtime team, advisory mode only, collect feedback and conflict metrics.
4) Week 11-14: Expand to console and API teams, add canary scheduling templates and cutover checklist exports.
5) Week 15+: Evaluate enforcement gates and capacity hedging automation for Dedicated customers.

Acceptance criteria:
- Reservation API exists and can be consumed by runtime pipelines.
- Console shows calendar with reservations, conflict markers, and suggestion panel.
- At least one pilot release uses DRTO advisory and logs telemetry to demonstrate conflict detection.
- Documentation and runbook for calendar owners and release stewards are published.

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: Teams ignore advisory recommendations leading to continued collisions. Mitigation: require visible risk acknowledgments on overrides and periodic governance reviews.
- Risk: Calendar becomes source of friction slowing launches. Mitigation: keep advisory only for MVP; invest in low-friction UI flows and API hooks.
- Risk: Capacity data freshness causes bad recommendations. Mitigation: ensure capacity syncs with Dedicated scheduler and add freshness indicators.

Artifacts and links:
- PRD: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1DRTO-PRD (internal)
- Design spec (console flows): https://drive.redwood/drive/design/drto-v1
- Pilot schedule spreadsheet: https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/DRTO-pilot
- Sample GitHub PR integration prototype: https://github.com/redwood-inference/drto-proto/pull/23
- Incident learnings relevant to scheduling collisions: https://confluence.redwood/incident/scheduling-collisions-2025

Next steps (short-term):
- Align pilot release owners and confirm pilot dates (action: Elena Park).
- UX mockups for reservation flow and conflict modal (action: Jonah Rivera).
- Runtime pipeline team to prototype reservation API client (action: Runtime Eng).
comments: 2026-03-02 — Priya: Scoping call with runtime + console completed. Runtime prefers advisory mode initially. Action: Glucose (Elena) to propose pilot launch in late April.
2026-03-04 — Jonah: Initial wireframes reviewed; suggested inline conflict resolution with one-click alternative slot booking. Will iterate on low-typing flow.
2026-03-06 — Elena: Runtime team will attach a lightweight client to release pipeline that calls reservation API and warns if no reservation exists. Prototype due end of week 6.
2026-03-08 — Samir (SRE): Security review note — ensure audit logs and KMS usage for calendar changes in Private deployments. Scheduling metadata must be logged to existing audit stream.
2026-03-10 — Marco: Added project-level estimate and updated acceptance criteria. Scheduling templates for canaries drafted; next: stakeholder signoffs.
acceptance_criteria: Reservation API callable from CI/CD pipelines and returns conflict status
Calendar UI displays reservations, conflict suggestions, and canary templates
Pilot release uses DRTO advisory and emits conflict telemetry to Launch Orchestra dashboard
Owner runbook and escalation path documented in Confluence

4) Week 11-14: Expand to console and API teams, add canary scheduling templates and cutover checklist exports.
5) Week 15+: Evaluate enforcement gates and capacity hedging automation for Dedicated customers.

Acceptance criteria:
- Reservation API exists and can be consumed by runtime pipelines.
- Console shows calendar with reservations, conflict markers, and suggestion panel.
- At least one pilot release uses DRTO advisory and logs telemetry to demonstrate conflict detection.
- Documentation and runbook for calendar owners and release stewards are published.

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: Teams ignore advisory recommendations leading to continued collisions. Mitigation: require visible risk acknowledgments on overrides and periodic governance reviews.
- Risk: Calendar becomes source of friction slowing launches. Mitigation: keep advisory only for MVP; invest in low-friction UI flows and API hooks.
- Risk: Capacity data freshness causes bad recommendations. Mitigation: ensure capacity syncs with Dedicated scheduler and add freshness indicators.

- PRD: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1DRTO-PRD (internal)

- Runtime pipeline team to prototype reservation API client (action: Runtime Eng).
text:
Objective: Build a Dynamic Release Train Orchestrator (DRTO) that provides a single authoritative launch calendar and automated conflict-resolution layer for runtime, console, and API releases. DRTO should allow teams to reserve launch slots, attach canary windows, declare blackout windows and capacity reservations, and automatically compute safe stagger policies and fallback lanes when conflicts are detected.

Background: Launch friction has been a recurring source of latency and outages when multiple product teams schedule large runtime or console changes in overlapping windows. The current playbooks are manual (spreadsheets + Slack) and cause avoidable collisions, overbooked GPU capacity, and late-stage rollbacks. This initiative aims to reduce schedule conflicts, accelerate cross-team coordination, and provide automated signals for go/no-go decisions.

Scope (MVP):
- Central calendar service integrated into Redwood Console with read/write reservations for runtime, console, API releases.
- Basic conflict detection and advisory recommendations (suggest alternative slots, show capacity impact).
- Canary/cohort tagging and automatic canary window insertion for model-impacting runtime changes.
- Exportable cutover checklist generator and notification hooks for oncall and stakeholders.
- Integrations: GitHub PR metadata parsing (release tag), Jira/Linear link attachment, and a lightweight API to query capacity reservations.

Out of scope (MVP):
- Enforced scheduling locks that block deployments (will be considered after advisory pilot).
- Deep autoscaling automation tied to DRTO decisions (future).

Key stakeholders:
- Product: Priya Natarajan (owner)
- Runtime Engineering: Elena Park (lead)
- Console UX: Jonah Rivera (design lead)
- Platform Reliability: Samir Gupta (SRE review)
- Growth/Customer Ops: Hannah Lee (customer comms)

Design decisions and trade-offs:
- Advisory vs Enforced mode: MVP will be advisory only to avoid blocking teams during early rollout; enforcement can be added if advisory outcomes are proven reliable.
- Centralized calendar: pros = single source of truth; cons = potential bottleneck and increased coordination overhead. We'll mitigate with well-defined owner roles and delegated sub-calendars for large orgs.
- Automated slot recommendations will favor minimal model-impact windows for runtime upgrades (nighttime in region, low-traffic cohorts) but allow overrides with documented risk acceptance.

Metrics / Success criteria:
- Reduce schedule collisions (two+ major changes in overlapping capacity-sensitive windows) by 80% in first quarter after pilot.
- Decrease late-stage rollbacks attributable to scheduling conflicts by 50%.
- Mean time to resolve calendar conflicts (manual overrides reduced) under 24 hours.
- Adoption: 60% of runtime/console release owners use DRTO advisory for new releases within 3 months of pilot.

Operational plan and rollout:
1) Week 0-4: Implement calendar backend, reservation schema, and conflict detection engine.
2) Week 5-8: Console UI integration (read/write) + GitHub/Linear linking and notifications.
3) Week 9-10: Pilot with runtime team, advisory mode only, collect feedback and conflict metrics.
4) Week 11-14: Expand to console and API teams, add canary scheduling templates and cutover checklist exports.
5) Week 15+: Evaluate enforcement gates and capacity hedging automation for Dedicated customers.

Acceptance criteria:
- Reservation API exists and can be consumed by runtime pipelines.
- Console shows calendar with reservations, conflict markers, and suggestion panel.
- At least one pilot release uses DRTO advisory and logs telemetry to demonstrate conflict detection.
- Documentation and runbook for calendar owners and release stewards are published.

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: Teams ignore advisory recommendations leading to continued collisions. Mitigation: require visible risk acknowledgments on overrides and periodic governance reviews.
- Risk: Calendar becomes source of friction slowing launches. Mitigation: keep advisory only for MVP; invest in low-friction UI flows and API hooks.
- Risk: Capacity data freshness causes bad recommendations. Mitigation: ensure capacity syncs with Dedicated scheduler and add freshness indicators.

- UX mockups for reservation flow and conflict modal (action: Jonah Rivera).
- Runtime pipeline team to prototype reservation API client (action: Runtime Eng).

source.title: initiative-level-staffing-buffer-policy-and-governance
source.creator: Sofia Marin
source.description:
Background: As we scale Redwoods product footprint and expand Dedicated/Private offerings, cycle plans are encountering recurring over-commitment and churn driven by reactive engineering requests, infra incidents, and late-requested compliance work. Current ad-hoc buffers (team-level fudge factors) lead to inconsistent treatment across initiatives and surprise scope cuts mid-cycle.

Goal: Define a repeatable, initiative-level staffing buffer policy and lightweight governance process that: 1) reduces mid-cycle scope volatility, 2) aligns expectations between PM/Eng/Design, and 3) preserves runway for high-likelihood emergent work. Policy should be actionable in planning workflows and instrumented in the Cycle Capacity Simulator.

Proposed policy (summary):
- Buffer sizing: baseline buffer = max(10% of raw estimated effort, 1 velocity point per 2 engineers) + risk-adjustment band (0%/5%/10%) based on initiative risk score.
- Risk scoring dimensions: third-party dependencies, infra changes, security/compliance scope, cross-org dependencies, new platform surface area. Each axis scored 0-2; higher totals map to risk-adjustment band.
- Buffer allocation: buffer is applied at initiative level and held as a reserve pool that PMs can request during cycle via a simple justification workflow; unspent buffer is returned to capacity at cycle end and tracked in velocity metrics.
- Governance: Planning Advisory Panel (PAP) to review buffer requests >50% of baseline buffer or >2 velocity points. Requests <=50% auto-approved but logged for review.
- Transparency and tooling: surface buffer as a separate line in the Cycle Planning template and in the Consoles cycle dashboard; add buffer controls to the Cycle Capacity Simulator so teams can model outcomes.

Success metrics:
- Reduce mid-cycle scope cuts by 40% (baseline measured over last 6 cycles).
- Decrease unplanned high-priority spillovers to backlog by 30%.
- >80% of buffer requests resolved within 3 business days during pilot.

Rollout plan:
1) Pilot (Cycle 2026-W11): infra and console initiatives adopt policy; PMs log buffer requests in a dedicated sheet and use Cycle Capacity Simulator.
2) Iterate (2 cycles): refine risk scoring weights and auto-approval thresholds from pilot learnings.
3) Full rollout (Cycle 2026-W15): update planning templates, add UI affordance in the Console, and train PMs/PMEs.

Implementation workstreams:
- PM: finalize policy language, training materials, scoring rubric.
- Eng: wire buffer accounting into cycle planning dashboards; add simulation knobs.
- Design: update planning template and dashboard UI copy.
- Data/Analytics: baseline metrics and a dashboard for buffer usage and outcomes.

Acceptance criteria:
- Policy doc approved by Product leadership and Eng leadership.
- Cycle Capacity Simulator updated with buffer model and a sample simulation for pilot initiatives.
- Pilot teams confirm workflow and log at least one buffer request (approved or denied).

Open questions:
- How do we treat cross-team buffers when the originating team cannot consume their allocation? (Fallback: PAP decides within 72 hours, with a defined escalation path to the Head of Product.)
- Should the buffer be bill-of-work (specific tasks reserved) or generic capacity that can be applied to any emergent priority?
- Metrics ownership: who will own the post-cycle buffer retro and reporting?

Notes: This policy intentionally avoids large fixed reserves which inflate committed scope; instead it focuses on a predictable, governed reserve with clear visibility.
comments: 2026-02-24 - Ethan Park: Drafted initial policy after capacity workshop. Uploaded PRD draft: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/PM/Cycle+Buffer+Policy+Draft
2026-03-01 - Isabel Chen (Eng Director): Suggested tightening auto-approval threshold to 50% and adding an infra-exception flag for incident-fueled buffer draws.
2026-03-03 - Data Team (note): Baseline: across last 6 cycles average mid-cycle scope cuts = 18%. Will validate after pilot. Query: https://dashboard.redwood.ai/queries/cycle-buffer-baseline
2026-03-05 - Design (Maya Patel): Uploaded updated planning template mockups. File: https://drive.redwood.ai/drive/planning-templates/cycle-buffer-mockup_v2.pdf
2026-03-07 - Ethan Park: Scheduled PAP review meeting for pilot approval (2026-03-11). Calendar invite: PAP-Buffer-Review. Added implementation ticket ENG-19845 for simulator knobs.
2026-03-10 - PAP decision note: Pilot approved for infra & console initiatives. Action: Eng to prioritize simulator changes (ENG-19845) and PMs to use spreadsheet tracker for requests.

source.title: PRD: Temporal Credit Faucet & Rapid Burst Reconciler for Dedicated Capacity
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Problem statement: Customers using Dedicated capacity report two recurring pain points: (1) short-lived traffic spikes cause failed requests because reserved pools are fully utilized and prewarm latency for new workers is high, and (2) tenant isolation is brittle when burst policies allow lateral preemption or aggressive backfill that impacts SLA-critical tenants. Goal: Design a system-level PRD that introduces a temporal credit faucet (small, time-limited emergency credits) plus a rapid burst reconciler (fast backfill + non-disruptive prewarm handoff) to reduce 5xx rate during spikes and preserve SLAs for priority tenants. This PRD covers API surface, console UX, autoscale orchestration behavior, observability, rollout strategy, and acceptance criteria.
goals: Reduce failed requests (5xx/429) during transient spikes by 60% for affected tenants
Provide emergency short-duration capacity credits that can be consumed without modifying tenant reservation contracts
Ensure credits are gated by safety checks to prevent noisy-tenant amplification
Allow rapid backfill (sub-30s) to warm workers for burst traffic without evicting higher-priority reservations
Expose clear console controls and telemetry for admins to inspect credit usage and reconciler activity
non_goals: Replacing long-term reserved capacity or changing contract billing models
Full redesign of the eviction and long-term preemption system
Support for on-prem air-gapped deployments in the initial launch (will follow in dedicated-private roadmap)
api_changes: POST /v1/dedicated/tenants/{tenant_id}/credit-faucet - request temporary credit with parameters: duration_seconds, max_tokens_units, safety_profile_id
GET /v1/dedicated/tenants/{tenant_id}/credit-faucet - returns active credits and usage history
PATCH /v1/dedicated/tenants/{tenant_id}/burst-reconciler - control flags: enable, prewarm_mode (fast|graceful), max_backfill_nodes
Events emitted: credit_issued, credit_consumed, reconciler_backfill_started, reconciler_backfill_completed
ui_changes: Reserved Pools view: new column 'Temporal Credits' showing remaining credits and last-issue time
Tenant detail: 'Burst' tab with a timeline of credit events, backfill attempts, and prewarm traces
Operations Console: a control pane to manually revoke or grant credits with audit logging
Runbooks accessible from incident panel when reconciler triggers create latency anomalies
rollout_plan: Phase 0 (Canary): Internal infra tenants only, credit faucet capped at 2k token-equivalents and duration 60s
Phase 1 (Beta): Select customers opt-in via console toggle; monitoring enabled and alerts to capacity-oncall
Phase 2 (Gradual): Auto-enablement for low-risk customers with limited daily budget and visibility defaults off
Phase 3 (GA): Public API enabled, docs published, billing and SLA language updated
acceptance_criteria: AC1: API - Issuing a credit through POST returns 200 and an id; subsequent GET shows the credit and consumed units
AC2: Safety - The system rejects credit issuance when tenant is on 'blocked_faucet' list or when cross-tenant contention metric > threshold
AC3: Autoscale behavior - Reconciler triggers backfill and prewarm within 30s of credit consumption for 90th percentile of simulated spike traces
AC4: Isolation - No tenant with 'priority:critical' should see >5% increase in P95 latency due to reconciler backfill operations during canary runs
AC5: Observable - Docs and console show lineage for credit events and reconciler runs; at least 3 dashboards (credits, reconciler-latency, safety-hits) publish within the release
AC6: Rollback - Feature flags allow immediate global disable of faucet and reconciler within 120s from a control-plane command
AC7: Auditability - All faucet operations are recorded to audit logs with actor, reason, and correlated request ids
telemetry_and_slo: Reliability metrics: 5xx_rate and P95 latency for priority tenants during reconciler activity
SLO target for canary: reconcile-induced-5xx delta < 0.2% absolute
tasks: Spec: finalize API schema and safety profiles - owner: PM (Ava) - due: 2026-02-28
Engineering spike: simulate 4 typical spike shapes and measure warmup times - owner: ENG - due: 2026-03-07
Runtime: implement faucet token accounting and short-lived assignment - owner: ENG Infra - due: 2026-03-21
Console: design and add tenant Burst tab and control panel - owner: Design (Marina S.) - due: 2026-03-28
Telemetry: add dashboards and alert rules; instrument events - owner: Observability - due: 2026-04-04
Security: review audit logging and KMS usage for faucet operations - owner: Security - due: 2026-03-18
Docs: draft API reference, runbooks, and admin guide - owner: DevRel - due: 2026-04-10
QA: create chaos tests that flip feature-flag and simulate noisy tenants - owner: QA - due: 2026-04-15
Release: create gated rollout plan and feature-flag strategy - owner: PM (Ava) - due: 2026-04-20
decisions: D1: Credits are time-limited (default 60s) and measured in token-equivalents to align with billing primitives
D2: Faucet consumption is local to zone by default to avoid cross-zone cold-starts, with global override for emergency ops
D3: Reconciler will prefer non-preemptive warmups (fast prewarm) but allow constrained preemption under configurable safety profiles
D4: Safety profiles are tenant attributes (low/medium/high) that tune faucet caps and backfill aggression
tradeoffs: T1: Allowing faucet credits risks amplifying noisy-tenant spikes vs. eliminating customer-visible failures; mitigated via safety checks and daily budgets
T2: Fast prewarm reduces tail latency but increases transient resource usage (cost); we accept mild cost increase during emergencies in exchange for availability
T3: Local zone-only credits reduce cross-zone contention but complicate global fairness guarantees
comments: 2026-02-18 - Ava Chen: Initial draft posted; requested eng spike to validate 30s backfill target (added task).
2026-02-26 - Eng Lead (Priya): Spike results show median backfill 18s, 95th at 52s for worst-case model; recommends prewarm parallelism cap of 4.
2026-03-02 - Design (Marina S.): Mockups uploaded; UX suggests 'Reconcile now' manual button in tenant ops. Link in attachments.
2026-03-05 - Security (Noah): Audit log schema approved; KMS envelope covered. Asked to document threat model for credit issuance.
2026-03-09 - Capacity Oncall (Diego): Requesting conservative defaults in canary to avoid noisy-tenant escalation during business hours.
2026-03-10 - PM (Ava): Updated acceptance criteria and added rollback time requirement per operations feedback.

source.title: OKR Horizon Snapshot & Decision Tree for Quarterly Capacity Allocation
source.creator: Maya Thompson
source.description: Goal: produce an operational playbook and an evidence-backed decision tree to convert high-level Company OKRs into a prioritized set of cross-functional initiatives and concrete capacity allocations for the quarter. Scope: consolidates inputs from GTM, Engineering, Platform, and Research; defines a 3-axis scoring rubric (impact, cost-to-implement, execution-risk); outputs a ranked initiative list, hedged capacity plan (reserved/burst/hosted mixes), and escalation lanes for resource conflicts. Deliverables: 1) OKR Horizon Snapshot PDF, 2) Decision Tree workbook (sheet + scoring), 3) Runbook for quarterly rebalancing and emergency reallocation, 4) Guardrails for Dedicated vs Hosted trade-offs.
acceptance_criteria: Decision tree documented with clear thresholds for OKR->initiative assignment
Scoring rubric defined and applied to at least 12 candidate initiatives across teams
Capacity allocation plan (reserved vs. hosted vs. burst) with cost estimates and risk notes
Stakeholder approvals recorded from Eng, Platform, Security, and GTM leads
Playbook contains an actionable escalation path and one worked example (critical incident & surge)
comments: 2026-02-10 Maya Thompson: Created ticket after leadership sync; initial outline attached; asked Eng/Platform to nominate 6 initiatives for scoring.
2026-02-17 Ethan Park: Collected initiative briefs from Platform (3), Console (2), and Research (1). Added initial estimates for infra effort and expected latency SLO impact.

2026-02-22 Priya Singh (Engineering): Reviewed scoring rubric — requested adding a 'rollback complexity' factor to execution-risk; noted that quantization work can reduce cost but increase near-term risk.
2026-02-25 Lila Moreno (Security): Confirmed no immediate security gating for proposed initiatives; recommended KMS integration item be separated as dependency.
2026-02-28 Ethan Park: Completed first-pass scoring spreadsheet (sheet link in artifacts). Top 3 initiatives account for 68% of projected Q1 infra delta.
2026-03-03 Cross-functional review: GTM raised concern about deprioritizing two feature experiments; we recorded trade-offs and created a mitigation plan (short A/B test runway).
2026-03-06 Maya Thompson: Incorporated feedback into the playbook; added a capacity hedging strategy (20% burst buffer + auto-fallback to lower-cap model variant).
2026-03-09 Engineering leadership: Approved the decision thresholds for green/amBER/RED allocation. Requested a canary policy for model variant fallback (linked in artifacts).
2026-03-10 Ethan Park: Submitted for executive review. Action: capture final signoff and publish to Confluence; schedule handover to Program Ops for quarterly cadence.
decision_notes: We selected a 3-axis rubric (impact / implementation-cost / rollback-complexity) after side-by-side scoring of 18 candidate initiatives — this balanced short-term revenue bets against long-term platform stability.

Capacity hedging: recommend 60% reserved (Dedicated) for baseline predictable workloads, 20% burst for seasonal spikes, 20% hosted for experiments and low-risk growth — rationale documented in workbook.
Trade-off: aggressive quantization on low-latency models reduces cost by ~35% but increases rollback complexity; decided to pilot quantization on one less-customer-facing model variant first.
Escalation: any initiative that consumes >30% of planned infra delta must trigger a capacity review board (Eng VP, Platform Lead, PM Lead) within 48 hours.

source.title: Modular retention templates and automated evidence playbook for request data
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Goal: Provide a modular, policy-driven retention template system for prompts/responses/request logs/metrics plus a standardized automated evidence playbook that can produce compliance-ready export bundles for audits. The feature should make it simple for customers to pick or customize retention profiles (examples: GDPR-Short, HIPAA-Long, EU-Residency-Only), visualize effective retention per route/tenant, and produce signed evidence packages with metadata and provenance for auditors.

Scope:
- Authoring UI in Console for 'Retention Templates' (create, clone, compose).
- Template primitives: data classes (prompts, responses, request-metadata, logs, metrics), retention TTL, residency constraints, anonymization/transformation rules, and evidence rules (export cadence, bundle format, signatures).
- Template inheritance: org -> project -> route overrides with clear effective retention resolution and conflict warnings.
- Evidence playbook: on-demand and scheduled export that produces a time-bounded bundle (ndjson + manifest + signed hash) with redaction/audit-log artifacts, plus an export audit entry stored for 1 year.
- Backend APIs for enforcement (GET effective retention, PATCH route retention, POST export-run).

Out of scope (this ticket): deep storage lifecycle plumbing for every storage provider; we will surface the intent and produce export bundles but actual cross-region archival connectors are separate work (follow-up ENG tasks).

Principles and constraints:
- Templates should be composable and human-readable (avoid opaque rule blobs).
- Default templates shipped for common regimes but always opt-in for residents.

source.title: Automated eval triggers for catalog and customer-locked model upgrades
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Goal: implement automated evaluation triggers that run targeted quality and safety suites whenever a model version is pinned, unpinned, or upgraded — for both catalog-managed models and customer-locked (pinned) models. This ticket defines the product scope, acceptance criteria, rollout policy, and required guardrails for the feature.

Why: We continue to see manual pin upgrades cause regressions in customer apps. Running lightweight, focused evals as a preflight and post-deploy step will reduce undetected regressions and enable safer rollouts. The system must be configurable (which suites to run), cost-aware (avoid runaway eval costs), and actionable (clear pass/fail gating and rollback recommendations).

Scope:
- Trigger points: catalog model version changes, customer pin upgrades, config diffs that affect runtime (quantization, tokenization, prompts-as-config).
- Eval types: smoke (latency, basic correctness), regression (production prompt set diffs), safety (toxicity/hallucination heuristics), and fidelity (embedding drift).
- Execution modes: preflight (blocking canary), post-deploy monitoring (non-blocking), and manual re-run.
- Policies: configurable thresholds per customer or global defaults; catalog upgrades will run preflight by default; customer-pinned upgrades will default to opt-in for self-serve customers and mandatory for enterprise with SLA.

Acceptance criteria:
1) When a catalog model version is promoted, the system queues a preflight eval job and reports results in the Console rollout UI with pass/fail status and metric diffs.
2) Admins can specify which eval suites run per model or per customer, with sensible defaults.
3) Failing preflight blocks canary rollout and surfaces mitigation options (rollback, reduced temperature/quant, alternate model).
4) All triggered eval runs appear in the Evals dashboard and are linked to the model-pin event, PR, and any ongoing rollout.
5) Alerts created for P0 regressions (e.g., >5% accuracy delta on critical prompt sets) notify on-call and the release owner.

Design & decisions so far:
- We will use ephemeral eval runners (separate from the hosted inference fleet) to avoid noisy neighbor effects on production. Pros: predictable cost accounting and isolation. Cons: additional infra cost; mitigated by scheduling and sample-size heuristics.
- Default eval sample sizes will be small for initial preflight (30–100 prompts) and larger for post-deploy (1k prompts over 24h) to catch regressions with statistical power.
- Catalog vs customer behavior: catalog upgrades run automatically; customer-pinned changes are opt-in except for customers on enterprise plans (decision based on security/compliance needs).
- Failure thresholds: conservative by default (e.g., >10% regression in utility metrics for non-critical suites, >3% for critical prompt sets). Thresholds are configurable per-customer and tied to the rollout policy.

Implementation plan (high level):
1) API and events: extend Catalog & Pin APIs to emit structured events (model_pin.change, model_pin.upgrade) with metadata about config diffs. (ENG- task will own schema)
2) Orchestration: build Eval Triggerer service to translate events to eval runs. Support tags to select eval suites.
3) Evals infra: add ephemeral runner provisioning and budgeting controls; integrate with existing eval harness.
4) Console: show preflight results in rollouts page, add one-click accept/rollback from results, and surface metric deltas and example prompt outputs.
5) Alerts & Integrations: wire failing preflight to PagerDuty and create GitHub PR comments linking to failed evals for model PRs.

Metrics of success:
- 50% reduction in post-upgrade regression incidents for catalog upgrades in first quarter post-launch.
- >80% coverage of catalog upgrades with preflight runs.
- <10% false-positive blocking rate (teams can override)

Dependencies: ["ENG-13550","PM-11024","ENG-2411"]

Links & artifacts:
- PRD: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/QA/Automated+Eval+Triggers
- Design notes: https://drive.redwood.ai/drive/eval-triggers/design-v1.pdf
- Eval harness repo: https://github.com/redwood-inference/eval-harness (PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/eval-harness/pull/3124)
- Console mockups: https://figma.com/file/abcd1234/rollout-eval-ui
- Incident reference: https://internal.redwood.ai/incidents/2025-09-upgrade-regression

Open trade-offs / questions:
- Cost vs coverage: how many sample prompts for preflight vs post-deploy (proposal: tiered sample size by customer plan).
- Opt-in behavior: whether self-serve customers should have automated preflight off by default. Initial product decision: automated for catalog; customer pinned auto-run only for enterprise.
- Runtime-sensitive config diffs: which diffs should trigger mandatory preflight (proposal: quantization level change, tokenizer changes, model architecture swap).

Next steps:
- ENG to prototype Event->Triggerer and ephemeral runner (sprint 2026-W10).
- PM to publish customer-facing docs + opt-in UI copy.
- Launch plan meeting w/ Sales & Solutions to define how we surface to enterprise customers (target: 2026-03-24).
comments: 2026-02-20 - Diego Morales: Kickoff with ENG and Research. Agreed on ephemeral runners for isolation. Next: small Spike to measure cost per preflight run.
2026-02-25 - Research (Maya Patel): Ran 3 sample suites across 4 models. Median preflight cost estimated at ~$12/run using 50 prompt sample. Sharing dataset in drive.
2026-03-02 - Design (Ethan Park): Added Console mockups for preflight results; included one-click rollback and view-failed-examples. Feedback: include time-to-run estimate in UI.
2026-03-05 - Security (Lina Gomez): Confirmed no additional cryptographic review needed for eval artifacts; data retention policy must be 30 days by default.
2026-03-09 - Diego Morales: Implementation plan drafted; ENG spike scheduled for 2026-W10. Added dependency ENG-13550 for event schema.
2026-03-10 - Ava Chen: Updated acceptance criteria to require Console linkbacks and PagerDuty for P0 regressions.

source.title: Rolling capacity triage guardrails and scope reserve
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Background:
Redwood's growth and increasing enterprise demand are stretching cycle capacity. Teams report mid-cycle churn from unplanned incidents, urgent customer escalations, and cross-functional rollouts which erode committed scope. We need standard, repeatable guardrails for assigning a rolling scope reserve and triaging mid-cycle work so that delivery predictability improves without blocking critical incidents.

Goal:
Define a lightweight, repeatable process and decision rubric that: 1) allocates a configurable reserve of team capacity each cycle; 2) standardizes triage steps for mid-cycle requests (feature changes, production incidents, partner needs); 3) provides explicit rules for scope cuts and redeployment of reserves during surges.

Success metrics:
- Cycle commitment hit rate (stories completed / stories committed) increases by 20% within two cycles after rollout.
- Mean unplanned work hours per engineer per cycle falls by 35%.
- Number of emergency roll-forward items requiring cross-team rework falls by 50%.

Proposed approach (high level):
1) Capacity Reserve Model: teams pick one of three reserve profiles when finalizing cycle commitments: 
   - Conservative (10% reserve): recommended for teams with high external dependencies or production-facing services.
   - Balanced (6% reserve): default for most product teams.
   - Aggressive (3% reserve): for mature, low-dependency squads aiming for high throughput.
   Reserve is expressed as story-point buffer or FTE-days depending on team preference.

2) Triage Rubric for mid-cycle work (triage owner: on-call PM + Eng lead):
   - Severity assessment: Customer-facing outage (P0), customer-impacting bug (P1), internal process improvement (P2), feature change request (P3).
   - Routing: P0 -> immediate interrupt, P1 -> evaluate for reserve consumption (if within reserve, accept; else escalate to Product Council), P2/P3 -> backlog with promised review window (5 business days).
   - Cost of fix vs business impact: use simple ROI worksheet for P1 decisions.

3) Scope-cut ladder (how to reclaim capacity when reserve is exhausted):
   - Step 1: Pause low-risk stretch goals (label: stretch).
   - Step 2: Defer listed non-blocking stories with clear fallbacks (assign owners for follow-up).
   - Step 3: If still urgent, invoke cross-team trade with counter-offers (exchange runway for prioritized work).

4) Governance and tooling:
   - Add "cycle-reserve-profile" metadata to Linear commitments for reporting.
   - Dashboards in Console showing reserve utilization and unplanned work by team.
   - Weekly mid-cycle checkpoint meeting template (15 min) for red/amber/green status per team.

Out-of-scope:
- Overhauling team-level estimation methodologies.
- Mandating a single reserve percentage for all teams.

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: Teams underreport unplanned work to game metrics. Mitigation: integrate automated incident logs and corroborate with on-call entries.
- Risk: Cross-team dependencies make reserve choices ineffective. Mitigation: require dependency mapping during planning; escalate to platform capacity pool if multiple teams are impacted.

Implementation plan and milestones:
- Week 0 (planning): Finalize rubric and reserve profiles, ship Linear metadata field (ENG-22111).
- Week 1 (pilot): Run with 6 volunteer teams (mix infra, product, and platform).
- Week 3 (iteration): Gather pilot feedback, refine dashboard widgets and checkpoint template.
- Week 5 (rollout): Announce org-wide, include playbook and short training for PMs and ENG leads.

Deliverables:
- Playbook doc with decision rubric and cut ladder (Confluence link attached).
- Linear templates + metadata field definition.
- Console dashboard for reserve utilization and unplanned work.
- Training slide deck and 30-minute office hours session for PMs and eng leads.

Acceptance criteria:
- The reserve metadata is available in Linear for all teams.
- Pilot teams report usage of the triage rubric in at least two mid-cycle events.
- Dashboard shows reserve utilization data and flags teams >75% reserve consumption.
- Training materials posted and at least one session delivered before rollout.
acceptance_criteria: Reserve metadata field added to Linear and documented
Dashboard surfaces reserve utilization and unplanned work by team
Pilot with 6 teams completes at least one mid-cycle triage using the rubric
Playbook and training materials published
Checkpoint template adopted by PMs for weekly mid-cycle review

2026-03-03 — Luca Moreno (Eng): Reviewed. Need ENG to add Linear field; I can own a small PR to the Linear integration. Flag: dependency on ENG-22111 for metadata API changes.

2026-03-05 — Priya Singh (Platform PM): Suggest adding a cross-team runway pool option for major incidents; will raise at PM Council. Also recommended a 'reserve-swapping' flow for temporary rebalancing.
2026-03-07 — Liam Patel: Assigned to run pilot recruitment. Reached out to 8 candidate teams; 6 agreed (runtime, console, dedicated, infra, embeddings, evals). Scheduled pilot kickoff for 2026-03-11.

2026-03-10 — Engineering lead (Sofia Alvarez): Confirmed ENG will ship the Linear metadata field in the next sprint; created ENG-22111 to track work. Noted potential workload on infra for dashboard ingestion — request for 2 sprints.

   - Severity assessment: Customer-facing outage (P0), customer-impacting bug (P1), internal process improvement (P2), feature change request (P3).
   - Routing: P0 -> immediate interrupt, P1 -> evaluate for reserve consumption (if within reserve, accept; else escalate to Product Council), P2/P3 -> backlog with promised review window (5 business days).
   - Cost of fix vs business impact: use simple ROI worksheet for P1 decisions.

3) Scope-cut ladder (how to reclaim capacity when reserve is exhausted):
   - Step 1: Pause low-risk stretch goals (label: stretch).
   - Step 2: Defer listed non-blocking stories with clear fallbacks (assign owners for follow-up).
   - Step 3: If still urgent, invoke cross-team trade with counter-offers (exchange runway for prioritized work).

4) Governance and tooling:
   - Add "cycle-reserve-profile" metadata to Linear commitments for reporting.
   - Dashboards in Console showing reserve utilization and unplanned work by team.
   - Weekly mid-cycle checkpoint meeting template (15 min) for red/amber/green status per team.

Out-of-scope:
- Overhauling team-level estimation methodologies.
- Mandating a single reserve percentage for all teams.

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: Teams underreport unplanned work to game metrics. Mitigation: integrate automated incident logs and corroborate with on-call entries.
- Risk: Cross-team dependencies make reserve choices ineffective. Mitigation: require dependency mapping during planning; escalate to platform capacity pool if multiple teams are impacted.

Implementation plan and milestones:
- Week 0 (planning): Finalize rubric and reserve profiles, ship Linear metadata field (ENG-22111).
- Week 1 (pilot): Run with 6 volunteer teams (mix infra, product, and platform).
- Week 3 (iteration): Gather pilot feedback, refine dashboard widgets and checkpoint template.
- Week 5 (rollout): Announce org-wide, include playbook and short training for PMs and ENG leads.

- Playbook doc with decision rubric and cut ladder (Confluence link attached).
- Linear templates + metadata field definition.
- Console dashboard for reserve utilization and unplanned work.
- Training slide deck and 30-minute office hours session for PMs and eng leads.

Acceptance criteria:
- The reserve metadata is available in Linear for all teams.
- Pilot teams report usage of the triage rubric in at least two mid-cycle events.
- Dashboard shows reserve utilization data and flags teams >75% reserve consumption.
- Training materials posted and at least one session delivered before rollout.
text:
Background:
Redwood's growth and increasing enterprise demand are stretching cycle capacity. Teams report mid-cycle churn from unplanned incidents, urgent customer escalations, and cross-functional rollouts which erode committed scope. We need standard, repeatable guardrails for assigning a rolling scope reserve and triaging mid-cycle work so that delivery predictability improves without blocking critical incidents.

Goal:
Define a lightweight, repeatable process and decision rubric that: 1) allocates a configurable reserve of team capacity each cycle; 2) standardizes triage steps for mid-cycle requests (feature changes, production incidents, partner needs); 3) provides explicit rules for scope cuts and redeployment of reserves during surges.

Success metrics:
- Cycle commitment hit rate (stories completed / stories committed) increases by 20% within two cycles after rollout.
- Mean unplanned work hours per engineer per cycle falls by 35%.
- Number of emergency roll-forward items requiring cross-team rework falls by 50%.

Proposed approach (high level):
1) Capacity Reserve Model: teams pick one of three reserve profiles when finalizing cycle commitments: 
   - Conservative (10% reserve): recommended for teams with high external dependencies or production-facing services.
   - Balanced (6% reserve): default for most product teams.
   - Aggressive (3% reserve): for mature, low-dependency squads aiming for high throughput.
   Reserve is expressed as story-point buffer or FTE-days depending on team preference.

2) Triage Rubric for mid-cycle work (triage owner: on-call PM + Eng lead):
   - Severity assessment: Customer-facing outage (P0), customer-impacting bug (P1), internal process improvement (P2), feature change request (P3).
   - Routing: P0 -> immediate interrupt, P1 -> evaluate for reserve consumption (if within reserve, accept; else escalate to Product Council), P2/P3 -> backlog with promised review window (5 business days).
   - Cost of fix vs business impact: use simple ROI worksheet for P1 decisions.

3) Scope-cut ladder (how to reclaim capacity when reserve is exhausted):
   - Step 1: Pause low-risk stretch goals (label: stretch).
   - Step 2: Defer listed non-blocking stories with clear fallbacks (assign owners for follow-up).
   - Step 3: If still urgent, invoke cross-team trade with counter-offers (exchange runway for prioritized work).

4) Governance and tooling:

Out-of-scope:

- Mandating a single reserve percentage for all teams.

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: Teams underreport unplanned work to game metrics. Mitigation: integrate automated incident logs and corroborate with on-call entries.
- Risk: Cross-team dependencies make reserve choices ineffective. Mitigation: require dependency mapping during planning; escalate to platform capacity pool if multiple teams are impacted.

Implementation plan and milestones:

- Week 1 (pilot): Run with 6 volunteer teams (mix infra, product, and platform).
- Week 3 (iteration): Gather pilot feedback, refine dashboard widgets and checkpoint template.
- Week 5 (rollout): Announce org-wide, include playbook and short training for PMs and ENG leads.

source.title: Release documentation orchestration and stakeholder traffic control
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Objective: Produce coordinated, audience-tailored release artifacts (customer-facing changelog, admin changelog, internal one-pager, and exec summary) and orchestrate an efficient, auditable stakeholder review flow so the release communications can ship on the planned date without last-minute redactions or compliance issues.

Scope:
- Draft two customer-facing changelogs: 'Consumer' (short, benefits-focused) and 'Admin' (detailed breaking changes, migration steps, RBAC/KMS notes).
- Create an internal release brief for support, sales, and account teams including rollout schedule, anticipated tickets, and escalation contacts.
- Produce an executive summary (1-pager) for leadership and post-release metrics owners.
- Run a cross-functional signoff loop with Engineering, Design, Security, Legal, Docs, Solutions Eng, and GTM.
- Localize customer-facing changelogs for EN, FR, DE; queue to translation vendor after signoff.
- Ensure embargoed timeline and gated publish actions in the newsroom and product docs.

Key decisions so far:
- Split changelogs into Consumer + Admin to reduce noise for standard users and surface necessary operational details for platform customers.
- Use the pre-existing release template stored in Confluence and the redaction checklist (security/PII/legal) before publishing.
- Automate signoff reminders through Slack #release-signoff and the @changelog-bot; manual signoff is required from Security and Legal for any changes that touch audit/logging or encryption text.

Deliverables:
- customer-facing changelog (short) – google drive draft link in field below.
- admin changelog (detailed) – drive link below.
- internal brief (confluence) – link below.
- exec one-pager (PDF) – to be uploaded to drive after final review.

Risk / Mitigations:
- Risk: Late legal redaction requiring last-minute edits. Mitigation: have Legal triage window 48–72 hours before publish; tag the specific paragraphs that require legal review.
- Risk: Localization delays. Mitigation: freeze English final 72 hours before publish and send to vendor; use priority pass for critical languages.

Tools & links:
- Drafts: https://drive.google.com/example/release-2026-03-consumer (consumer draft), https://drive.google.com/example/release-2026-03-admin (admin draft)
- Internal brief: https://confluence.company.internal/release-playbooks/2026-03-brief
- Signoff tracking board: https://linear.app/redwood/board/release-signoffs
- GitHub PR for docs: https://github.com/redwood-inference/docs/pull/981
- Release notes template: https://confluence.company.internal/templates/release-notes-template

Owner: PM to coordinate content; Design to produce short visual changelog headers; Engineering to validate technical change descriptions and callouts; Legal & Security to approve text that references encryption, audit, or customer data handling.

Acceptance criteria: see acceptance_criteria field.
source.acceptance_criteria: 1) Final customer-facing changelog published to the docs site with correct product tags and links; 2) Admin changelog uploaded to enterprise docs and linked from internal brief; 3) Legal and Security explicit signoff recorded in the signoff board; 4) Localization queued and vendor ticket created; 5) Internal one-pager distributed to #customer-success and #sales-ops and a pre-send Q&A doc created.
source.rollout_plan: Stage 1 (Canary): Publish changelog to staging docs site and invite internal reviewers for 24h. Stage 2 (Soft publish): Post the customer-facing changelog with a beta banner and notify top customers via CSMs. Stage 3 (General): Remove beta banner, update public newsroom and send newsletter. For Dedicated/Private customers, coordinate account-level emails with Solutions Eng. Use automated fallback copy if legal signoff delays more than 6 hours past embargo window.
release_notes_drafts: exec: to be uploaded after review
comments: 2026-02-19 - Ava Chen: Created ticket and linked initial drafts. Reached out to Security for pre-read; scheduled 2026-03-04 slot for legal review.
2026-02-25 - Diego Martinez: Design assigned to produce a header and 'what's new' illustration. Added localization label and contacted vendor for rate estimate.
2026-03-03 - Priya Kapoor (Security): Pre-read complete; flagged encryption paragraph in admin changelog for legal review. Requested clarification on KMS wording.
2026-03-04 - Michael Rivers (Legal): Quick sync done; asked to remove client names in two examples and provided redaction guidance. Legal approval will be explicit signoff in the signoff board.
2026-03-06 - Diego Martinez: Incorporated legal edits and updated admin changelog. Pushed docs PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/docs/pull/981. Requested engineering SME review for accuracy.

2026-03-08 - Sienna Park (Engineering): Confirmed accuracy of throughput/latency numbers; suggested adding a note about graceful degradation fallback policy. Marked ENG-88421 as dependency for verifying telemetry links.

2026-03-09 - Diego Martinez: Design reviewed consumer draft; shortened copy per feedback and produced two variant headers for A/B test. Scheduled final signoff meeting 2026-03-11 10:00 PT with cross-functional leads.
2026-03-10 - Ava Chen: Updated ticket with final signoff checklist and embargo timeline. Moved to In Progress; still waiting on Legal final signoff (expected 2026-03-11 morning).

source.title: Security readiness gates and crypto control matrix for Private VPC deployment
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: PRD addendum that consolidates security and compliance acceptance gates specifically for Private (VPC / on-prem) deployments. Focus areas: SSO/SAML and SCIM provisioning, RBAC role model and enforcement, tamper-evident audit logging and retention, and customer keying via KMS/HSM. Establish a control matrix mapping requirement -> owner -> verification method -> sign-off authority, and include explicit rollback/fallback procedures and scope-change triggers that require re-certification.
scope_inclusions: SAML 2.0 IdP integration with SP-initiated and IdP-initiated flows
SCIM user/group provisioning and deprovisioning tests
RBAC roles: OrgAdmin, BillingAdmin, DeployAdmin, ReadOnly, AppOperator; enforcement across Console and Control Plane APIs
Audit logging: write-once shipping to customer-owned storage and platform-access logs with integrity checks
CMEK support via cloud KMS and optional HSM-backed keys for private deployments
Acceptance gate checklist and sign-off owners for each control
scope_exclusions: Managed SSO for hosted customers (outside private deployments)
Third-party SIEM integration beyond export hooks
Legacy API key migration flows for older customers
requirements_summary: SSO/SAML: Support IdP metadata upload, signed assertions, single logout, and enforce attribute-mapping policy for role assignment
SCIM: Support user create/update/delete; webhook failure handling and reconciliation report
RBAC: Enforce least privilege; role binding evaluable via policy engine; audit trail of role changes
Audit Logs: Immutable, append-only logs with per-tenant retention policies and tamper-evidence (hash chaining); export path to customer S3/GCS; retention configurable up to 7 years
KMS/HSM: CMEK with key rotation, key usage audit, BYOK onboarding flow, and HSM-backed signing for FIPS workflows
Operational: Clear runbooks for key compromise, SSO federation changes, and audit log recovery
acceptance_criteria: SSO: Successful end-to-end SAML login with three IdP vendors (Okta, Azure AD, PingFederate) including signed assertions and attribute-based role mapping verified via automated test harness
SCIM: Automated provisioning test shows create/update/delete and idempotent reconciliation run without manual fixes for 1000 synthetic users
RBAC: Role enforcement tests block privilege escalation scenarios; role-change audit event recorded and queryable via Console within 60s
Audit: Proof-of-integrity test (hash-chaining) passes for 30 days of synthetic traffic; logs exported to customer bucket and verified via checksum
KMS: CMEK workflow completes including key import (BYOK) and scheduled rotation; HSM-backed signing available in Private Airgap mode
Documentation: Private deployment security runbook and customer onboarding checklist published and linked in Console
Sign-off: Security, Compliance, Product, and Solutions Engineering each provide explicit written acceptance for their sections
control_matrix: Requirement: SAML signed assertions -> Owner: Identity-Infra -> Verification: E2E tests + IdP vendor matrix -> Sign-off: Security Lead
Requirement: SCIM provisioning -> Owner: Platform-Auth -> Verification: Reconciliation report + load test -> Sign-off: PM
Requirement: RBAC enforcement -> Owner: Console -> Verification: Policy engine unit + integration tests -> Sign-off: Eng Manager
Requirement: Audit immutability -> Owner: Observability -> Verification: Integrity test harness -> Sign-off: Compliance
Requirement: CMEK/HSM -> Owner: Crypto -> Verification: Key import and rotation demo -> Sign-off: Security
signoff_checklist: Security review completed and mitigations backlog < 5 medium/high issues
Compliance checklist validated for SOC2 scope and updated evidence locations
Customer key onboarding runbook validated with 2 SRE-run dry-runs
End-to-end smoke test with Okta and Azure AD passed in staging
Audit log export path tested with sample tenant and verified checksums
RBAC tests demonstrate no cross-tenant privilege escalation on 5 attack scenarios
rollout_plan: Stage 0: Internal alpha in Private dev environment with test IdPs and synthetic tenants
Stage 1: Pilot with 2 strategic customers (one cloud-KMS, one BYOK-HSM) for 4 weeks
Stage 2: Gradual rollout to new private customers with checklist gating
Rollback: Revert control plane changes and trigger emergency CMEK key revocation playbook; isolate impacted tenants and re-run acceptance tests
Post-deploy: 30-day monitoring window and weekly compliance evidence exports
dependencies: ENG-4387 (audit-log export hooks and checksum endpoint)
ENG-4590 (CMEK key rotation UI and API)
change_log: 2026-02-25: Narrowed initial rollout to VPC-only (removed hosted preview) after risk review - Liam P.
2026-03-02: Added HSM-backed signing requirement for air-gapped private deployments - Security review
2026-03-07: Increased audit retention options to 7 years following Customer Success ask (enterprise compliance)
open_risks: BYOK legal and export controls for HSM key import may delay pilots in APAC
Large-scale SCIM sync performance for >100k users unknown until benchmarked
Integration surface for customer-managed KMS across clouds needs additional automation
comments: 2026-02-22 - Maria Gomez (Security): Need explicit MFA requirement for OrgAdmin in private console; add to acceptance criteria.
2026-02-26 - Dan Nguyen (Solutions Eng): Recommend a customer onboarding checklist run by Solutions Eng before pilot sign-up; added to rollout tasks.
2026-03-05 - Priya Rao (Compliance): Confirm evidence retention locations and ensure audit log exports include tenant id and signing checksum.
2026-03-09 - Liam Patel (assignee): Addressed SCIM reconciliation failure handling; added retry/backoff and reconciliation report generation. Test cases planned for next cycle.

source.title: Decoupled Reserved + Burst Pricing Pilot for Dedicated GPU Capacity
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Objective: run a structured pricing and packaging experiment for Dedicated capacity that separates baseline reserved GPU commitments from short-term burst credits and autoscale tokens, and validate SLA tier willingness-to-pay among enterprise prospects. Deliverables: experiment matrix, pilot contracts for 3 customers, instrumentation dashboard for unit economics, legal playbook for contract/security questionnaire answers, and rollout recommendation.
source.background: Customers request predictable baseline throughput (reserved GPUs) but want capacity to handle occasional spikes without long-term overcommitment. Current bundles mix reserved capacity and burst in a single SKU, making price sensitivity hard to measure and complicating SSO/KMS contract language for Private deployments. We propose decoupling: baseline reservations (monthly/annual) + portable burst credits + short-duration autoscale pool that maps to SLA tiers.
acceptance_criteria: Experiment plan documented and approved by Finance & Legal
Three pilot customers contracted with new packaging (1 SMB, 1 mid-market, 1 enterprise)
Dashboard showing conversion funnel from trial to paid reserved commit and average burst utilization per customer
Unit economics calc: per-token cost, gross margin delta, and projected break-even for 12/24 month commitments
Legal security/QnA playbook drafted and signed off by Security team
success_metrics: Conversion rate from trial to reserved commit >= 12% for pilot cohort
Average monthly burst draw per customer within forecast +/- 25%
Net-new ARR attributable to pilot >= $250k annualized by pilot close
No unmitigated SLA breach incidents during pilot
Sales feedback score >= 4/5 on contract simplicity
source.experiment_plan: 1) Define 3 packaging variants: A (baseline-heavy): 80% reserved / 20% burst credits; B (balanced): 60% reserved / 40% burst; C (flex-first): 40% reserved / 60% burst with higher overage discount. 2) Price each variant at three commitment lengths (1 month, 12 months, 24 months) with stepped per-GPU-hour discounts. 3) Offer SLA tiers: Bronze (best-effort), Silver (99.9% uptime, 95th percentile latency target), Gold (99.95% uptime, 99th percentile latency target + priority capacity routing). 4) Map autoscale tokens to SLA tier: Gold gets reserved pre-warmed pool for short bursts. 5) Instrumentation: tag requests by customer, package_variant, SLA_tier, and autoscale_token usage; capture latency, token consumption, and cost per token. 6) Run pilot with 3 customers for 8 weeks and evaluate metrics. 7) Iteration: adjust pricing and burst economics based on observed elasticity and unit economics.
source.pricing_matrix: Variant A: baseline 10 GPU-hours/day reserved at $X/hour, burst credits sold in 1k-credit bundles at $Y per 1k; Variant B: baseline 6 GPU-hours/day, burst 4; Variant C: baseline 3, burst 7 with deeper discount on committed 24-months. Overage: when burst credits exhausted, autoscale fallback uses pay-as-you-go rate (1.5x standard token price for Bronze, 1.2x for Silver, 1.0x for Gold) plus optional guaranteed pre-warm capacity add-on priced as a percentage of reserved spend. Finance sheet linked: https://drive.redwood/example/pricing-pilot-finance (placeholder).
contract_qna_checklist: SOC 2 Type II report available and link to control mapping
Encryption at rest: KMS options documented (Redwood-managed KMS, Customer-managed KMS) with key rotation cadence
Network isolation: VPC peering and Private control plane architecture diagram
RBAC & SSO: SAML/SCIM onboarding playbook and sample role matrix
Audit logs: retention options and cost implications
Data residency: regional deployment options and proof points
Patch & vulnerability cadence: monthly kernel/OS updates and emergency patch SLA
Incident response: contact list, RTO/RPO for Dedicated customers by SLA tier
Pen test attestation and remediation backlog summary
Encryption in transit: TLS versions and cipher suites
Dedicated upgrade/migration assistance clause for on-prem customers
contract_playbook_snippets: Termination assistance: 60 days export assistance with agreed runbook for model weights and KV cache
Data ownership: explicit clause stating customer owns model inputs/outputs and Redwood may retain anonymized telemetry for product improvement
Preemption: define preemption policy for burst pools when region capacity constrained and express fallback rules
SLA credits: capped at 10% of monthly committed fee for repeated SLA breaches
Security add-ons: SOC/ISO evidence delivery cadence and additional fee for advanced audit support
unit_economics_notes: Estimate baseline unit cost: GPU-hour amortized infra + ops + licensing = $0.85 per GPU-hour (internal estimate)
Burst serving cost higher due to pre-warm and priority routing: ~1.4x baseline
Target gross margin on reserved commit >= 60% after discounts for 12-month commitments
Break-even for 24-month commitment assumed at 55% utilization of reserved capacity
risks_and_mitigations: Risk: Customers misuse burst credits leading to unexpectedly high infra costs. Mitigation: rate-limit burst draw per-minute and notifications at 50/80/95% thresholds.
Risk: Legal pushback on data export/termination clauses. Mitigation: offer optional professional services engagement for migration; template SOW included.

Risk: SLA promises require engineering changes to routing and pre-warm pools. Mitigation: scope engineering runway and include fallback policy that keeps user-facing availability while avoiding guaranteed low latency under extreme load.
Risk: Pricing complexity reduces sales velocity. Mitigation: provide 1-page sales cheat sheet and automated quote generator embedded in CRM
notes: Finance ask: model sensitivity to 10/20/30% higher burst consumption than forecast
Security wants automated evidence export in enterprise contracts for quarterly audits
Sales feedback: prioritize simple naming and avoid >3 SKUs per region to reduce quoting errors
next_steps: Finalize pricing tiers with Finance by 2026-03-12
Legal to draft two pilot contract templates (SMB & Enterprise) by 2026-03-19
Schedule kickoff with selected pilot customers and Solutions Eng by 2026-03-22
Instrumentation tasks assigned to ENG: tag schema and dashboard by 2026-03-29
comments: 2026-03-02 - Priya Kapoor: Reviewed experiment plan; suggest adding 'grace buffer' for first 30 days to avoid immediate churn due to misforecast.
2026-03-04 - Jordan Li (Legal): Drafting confidentiality-safe pilot agreement; will add required security QnA appendix.
2026-03-05 - Elena Morozova (Engineering): Need clarifications on pre-warm capacity accounting; opened ENG-19876 to scope runtime changes.
2026-03-08 - Mateo Rios: Confirmed pilot customers: BetaHealth (mid-market), VectorLabs (enterprise), Pixly.ai (SMB). Sales leads assigned.
2026-03-09 - Finance (Kendall Park): Completed preliminary unit economics; see linked Drive finance sheet. Recommend increasing Gold tier premium by 12% to preserve margin.
